title: Consensus-backed metadata rollup and reader-friendly changelog
preview:
Context and motivation: We onboard models from a variety of sources (author-uploaded manifests, CI-provided metadata, and discovery probes). Discrepancies between sources (missing fields, differing perf metrics, variant aliases) have caused noisy changelogs and occasional release regressions. This PR implements a deterministic "consensus rollup" step during intake that: 1) canonicalizes manifest keys and field formats; 2) merges performance profile vectors using a configurable aggregator (weighted median by signal confidence); 3) records provenance for each field so downstream systems can explain where a value came from; and 4) emits a concise, reader-friendly changelog "teaser" that groups changes into: metadata edits, perf deltas, and variant-level lifecycle events.

What changed (high level):
- New consensus rollup service (registry/rollup) that takes a set of manifests and returns a single canonical manifest plus a changelog fragment.
- Aggregator implementations: weighted_median (default) and fallback_mean (opt-in).
- Field provenance map: compact provenance stamps stored alongside canonical fields for traceability.
- Changelog formatter (registry/changelog/teaser) that outputs a short prose summary plus machine tags for UI tooling.
- Intake pipeline wiring: the onboarding task now calls rollup before persisting model versions.
- Tests: unit tests for canonicalization rules, aggregator edge cases, and an end-to-end integration test that runs a small sample intake and asserts changelog shape.

Commits (summary):
- 8a7b2c1: add consensus rollup service and initial aggregator APIs
- 7f92d4e: implement weighted_median aggregator and provenance stamps
- f03bd9a: changelog teaser formatter and grouping rules
- c4a1b2e: hook rollup into intake task; add integration test harness
- d2f4e51: docs: onboarding guide updates and examples
- 1b8fa6c: fix: handle variant alias collisions; add extra unit tests

Files touched (high level):
- registry/rollup/service.py (new)
- registry/rollup/aggregators.py (new)
- registry/changelog/teaser.py (new)
- registry/intake/handler.py (modified)
- tests/registry/test_rollup.py (new)
- tests/registry/integration_test_sample_intake.py (new)
- docs/registry/onboarding.md (modified)
- pkg/schemas/model_manifest.yaml (modified)
- scripts/sample_manifests/*.json (added examples)

Review conversation (high-level excerpt):
- Ben Carter: "Great direction. Please clarify the conflict-resolution priority list in the docs and add a test for missing confidence scores." (requested changes)
- Aisha Patel (author): "Added explicit priority table to docs and added a test that defaults missing confidence to 0.4. Also documented how to opt into fallback_mean." (addressed)
- Lina Morales: "Can we guarantee aggregator determinism across Python versions? Consider sorting keys before aggregation." (comment)
- Aisha Patel: "Added key sorting and a deterministic float reduction step; included a note and added a regression test: tests/registry/test_rollup.py::test_aggregator_determinism." (addressed)
- Tomas Ilyin: "LGTM after CI green; small nits inline about log level and a type hint." (approved)

CI summary: Initial run showed one flaky integration test (tests/registry/integration_test_sample_intake.py::test_end_to_end) failing on timeout in the mock persistence layer. I reran the job; subsequent run passed. All checks reported green: lint, unit tests, integration, and build. Coverage slightly decreased in an unrelated helper module (acceptable per ENG-1934).

Backward compatibility and risk: No user-facing API changes. The canonical manifest schema was minimally extended with an optional provenance map; schema validations accept the new optional field. Intake path is feature-flagged on rollout; default enabled for new onboardings. The rollup introduces deterministic merging rules to reduce churn in release notes and should decrease noise. If a downstream system depends on specific field values from a single source, we document how to opt-out and pin source priority.

Testing and validation: Unit tests cover aggregator edge cases (empty lists, single-source, conflicting confidences). Integration test runs sample intake with three synthetic manifests and asserts canonical output and a changelog fragment with expected grouped headings. Perf: aggregator runs in ~3–7ms for typical manifests (measured in CI microbenchmark script). Added alerts if rollup latency exceeds 200ms in production.

Docs and examples: docs/registry/onboarding.md updated with a short example showing input manifests and resulting teaser. Added scripts/sample_manifests to allow reproducing the integration test locally.

Merge outcome: Squash-merged to main on 2026-03-02 after approvals. Post-merge actions: 1) feature flag rollout started in staging; 2) we scheduled a small canary sweep to validate changelog quality on ~50 recent onboardings (ENG-2041).

Notes for release engineers: There is a small database migration to add the optional provenance column (backfilled as NULL). Migration script included: db/migrations/20260302_add_provenance_column.sql. Rollback is safe (column optional).

title: Contextual release pruner and variant signal hash
preview: Problem: onboarding pages and release notes for model variants accumulate many low-signal items that hide deployment risks. This PR implements a contextual release pruner that selects a small, high-signal subset of changelog fragments and pairs it with a compact variant signal hash — a deterministic, size-bounded digest derived from sampled telemetry features — to help reviewers and downstream tools quickly assess runtime affinity. Key ideas: (1) Variant signal hash condenses anonymized telemetry-derived features (sequence length distribution quantiles, common token-prefixes, average KV cache hit hint) into a stable 64-char hex digest. The hash is produced from deterministic sketching of reservoir-sampled telemetry aggregates so it is reproducible across uploads and small enough for manifests. (2) Release pruner ranks candidate changelog fragments by a hybrid score (telemetry-weighted impact + semantic novelty) and applies a length and redundancy-aware pruning pass to produce a 3-item short summary and optional extended list. Implementation details: sketches and hash generation live in registry/lib/signals/hash_sketch.go; pruning engine is in services/release/pruner.go with a preview REST endpoint at api/release_preview.go. The uploader CLI gained --publish-short-release to enable the pruned output; the registry UI shows both short and full views. Privacy and safety: telemetry inputs are pre-aggregated, bucketed, and clipped; no raw traces are exported. Backwards compatibility: new fields are additive; older clients ignore short_release and variant_signal_hash. Rollout plan: feature-flagged behind feature_flags.release_pruner, internal pilot for two weeks, then opt-in for partners. Metrics from pilot: reviewer acceptance rate for onboarding briefs rose from 68% to 91% and average onboarding read-time decreased by 40%.

title: Introduce compact stability ledger and change-contextualizer for snapshot diffs
preview: Motivation and summary: Large snapshot onboarding produced verbose profile blobs and brittle release notes; this PR provides a compact, append-only 'stability ledger' designed to store sparse stability signals and provenance per snapshot plus a change contextualizer that synthesizes concise release briefs from ledger deltas and sampled eval signatures. Implementation highlights: ledger implementation in registry/ledger/stability.py introduces a tight binary+JSON entry format keyed by snapshot hash ribbon (sha256_base58). Each entry keeps stability_token (low/medium/high), a small latency_histogram_bucketed summary, token_error_rate as a fixed-point string, and a truncated provenance string; typical entry size is <1KB. The diff serializer at registry/snapshots/diff_serializer.py emits compact delta blobs containing tensor fingerprints, model config deltas, and profile delta summaries (throughput, p50, p95). The serializer supports streaming ingestion and resumable uploads and uses streamed sha256 fingerprinting with an LZ4 compression path. The contextualizer in tools/changelog/contextualizer.go reads ledger deltas and sampled eval signatures and generates prioritized 2-4 sentence briefs that call out regression windows, likely causes, and suggested mitigation actions (e.g., try q4 quantization or revert specific tokenizer changes). Migration and rollout: scripts/migrate_ledger.sh provides an idempotent offline compaction pass that converts legacy verbose snapshot metadata into ledger entries; it is safe to run on read-replicas and gated to registry-admin IAM. The feature is behind registry.features.compact_ledger and will be enabled first for model-registry-internal and a small customer cohort for two weeks before broad rollout. Backwards compatibility: new lightweight API endpoint /snapshots/{id}/ledger returns the compact ledger entry alongside existing full profile for opt-in clients; no breaking changes to existing endpoints. Performance and security considerations: entries are intentionally small to remain indexable in our metadata DB; serializer uses streamed hashing and LZ4 to reduce upload cost; provenance strings are sanitized and truncated to avoid PII, and migration requires elevated IAM. Tests and verification: unit tests added: tests/test_stability_ledger.py and tests/test_diff_serializer.py. Integration harness in benchmark-lab/fixtures/snapshot-churn simulates heavy snapshot churn and validates brief quality metrics. Commits: commit 9a1f2d8 adds ledger core and serializer, commit 3c4b7a1 adds contextualizer and go bindings, commit e2b6c55 adds migration script and tests. Files changed at a glance: registry/ledger/stability.py, registry/snapshots/diff_serializer.py, tools/changelog/contextualizer.go, scripts/migrate_ledger.sh, docs/registry/ledger.md, tests/test_stability_ledger.py, tests/test_diff_serializer.py, benchmark-lab/fixtures/snapshot-churn/seed.yaml, tools/ci/ledger_pipeline.yml, examples/onboard/compact-ledger-example.json, Makefile updates. Review discussion summary: Marcus requested deterministic fingerprinting and a terse entry schema; author clarified the streamed sha256 approach and added a compatibility shim. Sofia asked for privacy guarantees; added sanitization and a note in docs. Devon requested a rollout plan and metrics; added brief_quality and storage_savings metrics and a gated flag. CI: initial run showed a flaky integration test in the harness; rerun after increasing ingestion throttles passed all checks. Merge details: approved by reviewers and merged via squash-merge to main. Release notes: "Add compact stability ledger and contextual changelog generator for snapshot diffs; opt-in API endpoint /snapshots/{id}/ledger; migration script for converting legacy snapshot metadata; feature gated behind registry.features.compact_ledger." Rollout checklist: feature flag, enable internal cohort, monitor brief_quality >=0.85 and storage savings >=40% over legacy blobs, then enable for all. Additional notes: contextualizer is intentionally conservative and surfaces suggestions, not automated rollbacks; storage team consulted on index sizing and approved columnar entry plan.

title: Introduce mini-prober and cost-signature autodoc for lightweight model intake
preview:
Context and motivation: Model onboarding at scale needs a lightweight way to capture representative cost signals (average sequence length, token distribution, cache-friendliness) without running full telemetry. This PR adds a small, safe prober that runs a bounded sampling workload during onboarding and produces a compact 'cost-signature' artifact. The platform uses the signature to offer conservative cost guidance and to insert an autodrafted changelog entry when a model is onboarded or updated.

What I changed (high level): Added a mini-prober library and CLI wiring, a cost-signature serializer embedded in model metadata, a release-drafter hook that uses the signature to create a simple cost blurb, and tests/fixtures. The prober is intentionally conservative: max 50 sample prompts, short sequence caps, no external dataset downloads. It runs only when --mini-probe is enabled or when the onboarding flow includes the 'probe' step.

Design notes: The cost-signature is a compact JSON line (stored base64 in metadata) with fields: avg_tokens_est, median_seq_len, kv-cache-hit-estimate, bucketed-token-dist, quant-suggest (none/8bit/4bit), and a heuristic cost-impact-class (low/medium/high). The heuristics are conservative and documented in scripts/onboard/README.md.

Files and areas touched: sources/onboard/mini_prober.py, cli/onboard.py (flag wiring), metadata/cost_signature.py, release/drafter/hooks/cost_autodraft.py, tests/test_mini_prober.py, docs/onboard/prober.md.

Testing and CI: Added unit tests for signature serialization, a small integration harness that runs in CI with mocked model weights, and a docs page. CI run passed all checks after a lint fix.

Rollout plan: Feature-flag in main; rollout gated behind feature 'mini_prober.experimental' and opt-in via CLI by default. After 2 weeks of telemetry and a manual review, flip the opt-in to recommended. Private installer will default to disabled.

Checklist:
- [x] Unit tests added
- [x] Integration harness added
- [x] Documentation in docs/onboard/prober.md
- [x] Release notes drafted and auto-attached

Merge: Squash-and-merge after CI green and at least two approvals.

title: introduce-release-lighthouse-and-versioned-metrics-extractor
preview:
Motivation: release notes generation has become a bottleneck during model onboarding. Teams repeatedly rewrite similar perf/context blurb templates and manually reconcile metric snapshots for each model variant. This PR introduces a "release lighthouse" extractor that synthesizes a candidate human-friendly release synopsis and a versioned-metrics artifact (time-stamped, normalized vectors) for each onboarding run so downstream docs and the Console can consume consistent, auditable release snippets.

Design summary: the feature adds a pipeline step (registry.ingest -> lighthouse.extract -> artifact.store) that: (1) samples keyed perf snapshots (latency P50/P95, tokens/sec, mem), (2) runs lightweight heuristics to surface regressions and noteworthy regressions, (3) generates a short prose synopsis with labeled highlights (throughput, footprint, notable regressions) and optional mitigation notes, and (4) emits a versioned JSON metrics artifact and a markdown candidate release blurb. The extractor is intentionally deterministic given the same sampling seed to make changelogs reproducible.

Checklist: - add extractor module and feature flag - persist versioned metrics under model artifact namespace - wire a Console preview endpoint for candidate blurb - add unit and integration tests for sampling, formatting, and schema validity - add Linear links and a short rollout plan

Testing and QA: unit tests cover sampling heuristics and formatters; integration tests run the pipeline with a small synthetic model profile and validate the produced artifact schema and a candidate blurb length/structure. Ran the full CI twice to catch flakiness in the perf-sampler; added a backoff in the sampler to address transient timeouts when contacting the benchmarks store. Staging rollout validated with a synthetic canary (ref: ENG-8132).

Backward compatibility & migration: produced metrics artifacts are versioned (v1 -> v2) with a compatibility shim in the registry reader so Console and downstream scripts continue to work. Consumers can opt in to the new prose blurb via a feature flag. No changes required to existing onboarding contracts.

Rollout plan: feature-flag gated on prod. First enable for internal curation only for two weeks, then open to all model intake flows. Add a follow-up PR to expose the blurb editor in the Console and to hook the artifact into release automation.

Related: ENG-8132 (release meta automation), ENG-9345 (perf sampling reliability), SUP-1213 (support ticket that motivated timeline).

Release notes (draft): Add release lighthouse: automatically generate candidate release synopses and versioned performance artifacts during model onboarding. Improves consistency of release notes and provides auditable metric snapshots.

title: Introduce onboard lineage map and autorelease anchor synthesizer
preview:
Problem: When onboarding models we lacked a machine-readable lineage artifact that connects model package uploads, profiling runs, and generated release notes. That made automated changelog synthesis brittle and required manual stitching for multi-variant releases. This PR adds two pieces: (1) a compact onboard lineage map that records upstream artifacts (source repo, dataset snapshot id, build hash, profiling snapshot id) and (2) an autorelease anchor synthesizer that produces a canonical release anchor and draft changelog entries based on lineage and profile diffs.

Design summary: The onboard lineage map is emitted as part of the onboarding pipeline and stored in the registry as a lightweight provenance document. It uses stable identifiers (dataset-snap, build-hash, profile-seed) and explicit relation types (built-from, profiled-by, variant-of). The autorelease anchor synthesizer consumes the lineage map and the model's performance profile snapshots to generate: a) a short human-facing release anchor (one-line summary), b) structured changelog notes (regressions, improvements, config changes), and c) a machine signal for the release gating pipeline. The synthesizer is conservative: it flags unknowns for manual review and emits confidence scores for each note.

Checklist implemented: 
- [x] Onboard step writes lineage map JSON (compact schema)
- [x] Registry API accepts lineage map and indexes provenance tags
- [x] Small migration to backfill lineage_uuid for recent uploads (best-effort)

title: add provenance-backed metadata enricher and performance-stamp publisher
preview: Motivation: When models are onboarded we lack a compact, auditable performance signature attached to the model metadata that can be consumed by release note automation and ops tooling. This PR introduces a provenance-backed metadata enricher that synthesizes canonical metadata fields (fingerprint, hardware-affinity hints, sample-derived latency/p90/cost stamps) and a separate performance-stamp publisher that emits a small, versioned artifact used by the changelog/autodraft hook to populate 'performance notes' in release drafts. Scope: 1) onboarding enricher service (onboard/enricher) that runs post-ingest to compute and attach a performance signature and provenance ledger entry; 2) publisher service (publisher/stamp_publisher) that signs and stores a compact JSON stamp in model artifacts storage and emits an event for release automation; 3) small CLI hook (hooks/autodraft_publisher) consumed by the changelog synthesizer to include the stamp in draft release notes; 4) unit + integration tests and docs. Design notes: the enricher uses existing sampling harness (ingest/probes) and a deterministic fingerprinting algorithm to ensure stamps are reproducible; the publisher attaches a provenance token and KMS-wrapped signature (optional via feature flag) so downstream workflows can assert authenticity. Backwards compatibility: stamps are optional metadata keys and will not block existing onboards. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout initially on the canary intake pipeline, then enabled by default after 2 weeks of telemetry. Checklist: - [x] core enricher implementation - [x] publisher that writes signed stamp to blobstore - [x] changelog hook to read stamps and synthesize a performance blurb - [x] unit and integration tests - [x] docs and runbook entry - [x] feature-flag gating and migration path Tests: added deterministic unit tests for fingerprinting and a small integration test that simulates probe ingestion and stamp emission. Security: signing flow is pluggable; by default uses a development key if KMS is not configured. Impact: improves fidelity of automated release notes and provides a verifiable performance artifact for platform teams. See related Linear items ENG-1428 and ENG-1492 for telemetry and rollout milestones.

title: Rollup compat-map and onboarding metadata adapter with changelog seed backfill
preview:
Motivation: We have overlapping compatibility entries across model variants that complicate release note generation and rollout heuristics. This PR introduces a compatibility rollup step that composes pairwise compat entries into a compact matrix and a lightweight metadata adapter that normalizes incoming onboard payloads (provider, quantization tags, variant hints) so downstream profile generation and changelog seeds are stable.

What this PR does: - Adds a compat-rollup module that deduplicates and collapses pairwise compat rules into a hierarchical matrix keyed by model family/version. - Adds metadata-adapter layer which runs as a normalization pass during onboarding and when re-ingesting legacy manifests. - Adds a changelog-seed backfill migration that synthesizes short human-friendly blurbs for recent onboardings so release assembler has content to surface. - Adds unit + integration tests for rollup correctness and performance regression guard.

Checklist: - [x] compat-rollup algorithm implemented and unit-tested - [x] metadata adapter schema + validator - [x] changelog seed migration + idempotency guard - [x] e2e test with release-assembler (mocked) - [x] perf microbenchmark against registry index

Migration notes: The backfill is non-destructive: it writes changelog seeds to the registry only when no seed exists. The migration is safe to run repeatedly. We added a short TTL for temporary debug flags and a dry-run mode for the migration to inspect candidate seeds. Operators should run the dry-run once, inspect output, then run the actual migration during a low-traffic window.

Testing: Unit tests cover edgecases (circular compat rules, missing variant metadata). Integration test simulates onboard -> rollup -> release-assembler flow and asserts stable release_notes output. Local perf runs show ~12% reduction in profile lookup latency for heavy variant graphs (N=200 variants).

Related: ENG-4981 contains design notes on the rollup heuristics, SUP-1123 is the support ticket that prompted standardized metadata for third-party picks.

If anything goes wrong: rollback is a two-step process: 1) disable the migration flag to stop backfills; 2) deploy a revert that bypasses the rollup and falls back to the previous pairwise compat API.

title: curation-playbook-and-changelog-translation-hook
preview:
Motivation: release notes and compact changelogs generated by the model registry are currently English-first and optimized for machine consumption. Customers and internal stakeholders have requested two things: 1) better human-readable, concise release blurbs that can be surfaced in the Console and email digests; 2) localized (i18n) variants of those blurbs so non-English engineering and product teams can review and approve model rollouts without translation bottlenecks. This PR introduces a lightweight curation playbook and a changelog translation/templating hook that sit in the onboard -> release-assembler pipeline.

What this PR does (high level):
- Adds a curation playbook module (src/curation/playbook.py) that codifies checks and heuristics used during model onboarding: perf delta thresholds, sample-based sanity checks, affinity/quantization compatibility hints, and human-review triggers. The playbook emits a small JSON-compatible report used by the release assembler.
- Adds changelog translation hook (src/release/translation_hook.py) which takes a canonical compact-change object and renders both: (a) short human blurbs (1-2 sentence, bullet-safe) and (b) localized renderings using a glossary and simple grammar templates. The translation hook is intentionally offline-friendly and deterministic (no external translation API calls at build time).
- Integrates the playbook/translation-hook into the release assembler pipeline (src/release/assembler.py) and exposes a new CLI command redwood-model-registry release preview --locale <locale> to generate localized preview snippets for reviewers.
- Adds sample-driven annotations: small sample sets (tests/fixtures/samples) and an efficient sampler that runs in CI to detect semantic regressions for onboarding candidates (if the semantic similarity drops > threshold we mark for manual review).
- Adds unit + integration tests and a small e2e preview job in .github/workflows/release-preview.yml that renders localized blurbs in CI for PRs touching model metadata or changelog templates.

Checklist (what I validated):
- [x] Playbook rules implemented and unit-tested
- [x] Translation templates + glossary + locale fallback implemented
- [x] CLI preview and integration with assembler
- [x] End-to-end test for a toy model onboarding -> preview flow
- [x] Docs: added short README and usage notes under docs/curation

Notes on design choices:
- We intentionally avoid a black-box MT step in CI; templates + glossary yield consistent, reviewable outputs. Teams can plug in managed MT later via a small adapter.
- Playbook emits a concise report (score + flags) rather than gating merges; gating is left to retention policies and human reviewers.
- The compatibility hints are heuristic (quant/affinity scoring) and are surfaced as hints (not enforced), since hardware mapping varies per customer deployment.

Related work: ENG-5421 (model release localization) and PM-88 (concise changelog UX). This PR is intentionally self-contained and additive to the assembler; it introduces no breaking API changes.

title: Expose canonical model+version identifiers for runtime telemetry
preview:
Context: perf-canary baselines and alerting are keyed per model. Today, runtime telemetry frequently emits an unstable identifier (human alias, internal deployment name, or a resolved artifact pointer) depending on routing and control-plane config. That causes baseline fragmentation (same model -> multiple time series) and false regressions during rollouts or alias updates. This PR adds a canonical, registry-owned model identifier to the Model Registry "resolve" response and ensures the identifier is suitable for telemetry and long-lived aggregation.

What this changes: 1) Introduces canonical identifiers: model_id (stable UUID), model_slug (stable string, e.g. "llama-3-8b-instruct"), and model_version (registry semantic version or registry version id) as first-class fields in registry responses. 2) Extends the ResolveModel API to return canonical_model and canonical_version alongside the artifact reference used for loading. 3) Adds a dedicated "telemetry_key" (string) intended for metrics labels: "<model_slug>@<model_version>" (e.g. "llama-3-8b-instruct@2025-11-15.1"). This is explicitly NOT a customer-facing name; it is guaranteed stable for the lifetime of a model version in the registry. 4) Adds audit logging when alias -> canonical mapping changes, to aid post-incident for any baseline shifts.

Compatibility and rollout: - Backwards compatible: existing fields remain unchanged. New fields are additive. - The intended consumer is runtime telemetry (see redwood PR-19193 / PR-19203 follow-ups). Runtime can start emitting telemetry_key immediately once it updates its client. - The telemetry_key is designed to be low-cardinality: slug + version only. No region, hardware, tier, customer, or deployment id.

Why this solves baseline stability: perf-canary baselines are stored as {model, version, runtime_version, sku, region?}. If "model" shifts between alias vs internal deployment name across rollouts, baselines drift or reset. Canonical slug@version ensures series continuity across routing changes while still differentiating real version updates.

Notes: - model_slug is immutable after a model is published (enforced in registry). If we ever need to rename, we will publish a new slug and treat it as a new model for baseline purposes. - model_version is immutable; version string is validated and normalized. - We purposely do not include quantization profile or runtime kernel tags here; those belong to runtime telemetry (and are handled in separate PRs).

Testing: - Added unit tests for version normalization and immutability constraints. - Added integration test for ResolveModel returning canonical fields through the public registry client. - Load-tested label generation to ensure we do not allocate on hot paths (string concat is done once per resolve).

Follow-ups: - Runtime adoption PR in redwood (emit telemetry_key as the canonical model label). - Backfill job: map historical alias-based series to canonical where possible (telemetry pipeline).

title: introduce-cohort-sample-manifests-and-watermark-backed-change-drafter
preview: Motivation: onboarding teams asked for smaller, targeted sample sets for release notes and regressions that map to customer cohorts (large-enterprise, mobile-first, helpdesk). In addition we need a way to produce concise changelog drafts that preserve sample provenance but remove identifying tokens: a watermark-backed drafting flow that ties a compact diff summary to a sampled manifest. Design: add a CohortSample manifest (schema v2) that declares sample cohorts, deterministic seed, and retention/privacy tags; implement a watermark drafters subsystem that can generate compact changelog teasers (one-line impact bullets + representative sample snippets) tied to a sample manifest hash and signature. The drafter uses K-anonymized snippet trimming and an optional token-redaction pipeline. Implementation: 1) manifest schema and validation (manifest/v2) + CLI preview (onboard-sample preview --manifest-file). 2) sampling worker that generates cohort-specific sample packs and stores lightweight fingerprints to the model registry storage backend. 3) watermark drafters service that consumes a manifest hash and emits a compact markdown-ready changelog snippet, with an attached provenance token for audit and rollback tooling. 4) compatibility checks added to the onboard pipeline: sample-aware performance baseline query and a lightweight eval probe that runs up to 100 queries across cohorts. 5) telemetry hooks so Console can show per-cohort sample coverage and digest generation latency. Rollout: behind feature-flag onboard.cohort_samples + gradual opt-in for model curators. Docs: CLI docs, manifest schema reference, and a short how-to for product teams to craft cohorts. Security/privacy: default redaction on snippets, signing of manifest hashes, and ability to opt-out of storing raw snippets. This PR groups the work into a logical change set to enable concise, cohort-aware release notes and safer public previews.

title: Introduce shingled onboard metadata and size-aware release extract
preview:
Background and motivation: onboarding very large model metadata bundles (profiles, test vectors, long-form provenance) has repeatedly caused large PR payloads and slow downstream release note generation. Large deltas were both noisy and expensive to store/serialize, and the release assembler often generated verbose changelogs that made it hard for release owners to triage. This PR introduces a shingled metadata serializer for onboard payloads (metadata shingling) and a size-aware release extract pipeline that samples and compresses changelog candidates to produce concise release notes. Goals: reduce payload size during model onboarding, make changelog summaries readable by humans, and provide deterministic, auditable release extracts for downstream automation.

What I changed:
- onboard/serializer: add shingle_encoder.py (shingled metadata encoding). The encoder splits large metadata maps into consistent "shingles" (content-addressed shards) keyed by a stable fingerprint. Shingles are stored alongside a compact manifest that references shingles with offsets and versioning metadata. This enables small delta uploads when only parts of the metadata change.
- onboard/cli: add --shingle-threshold flag to onboard CLI (defaults to 64KB per property) and a dry-run mode that emits a predicted shard manifest.
- registry/storage: add ShingleStore abstraction and an S3-backed implementation with reference-counted shard lifecycle and garbage-collection hooks. The ShingleStore exposes a small API used by the onboard pipeline to dedupe repeated payloads across models and versions.
- release/extract: add size_aware_changelog_sampler which reads profile deltas and picks representative metric changes with heuristics tuned to shard-level diffs. The sampler produces a prioritized list of up to N blurbs (configurable) and simulates a human-friendly sentence for each blurb.
- release/cli: add release-extract --summarize flag that creates a concise release_notes.json alongside the full changelog bundle.
- tests: unit tests for shingle_encoder edge cases, integration for full onboard flow (small model and large model with repeated metadata), and e2e test for release-extract summarization.
- docs: onboarding.md adds a section on shingling and guidance for when to enable it (enterprise customers with very large provenance).

Backwards compatibility: the shingled upload falls back to a legacy full-payload path when the registry is in compatibility mode; the manifest contains a version tag so older registries ignore shards until migrated. ShingleStore provides a compatibility loader that can rehydrate full metadata for older code paths.

Performance notes: local benchmarks included in this PR show ~3x reduction in upload bytes (median) for large profiles and a 40-60% reduction in changelog size for profiles with repetitive provenance entries. These numbers vary by profile shape; the sampler is conservative by default.

Security and cost: storing shingles increases object count in cloud storage; we added a GC policy and reference counting to avoid unbounded growth. Shingles are content-addressed and immutable; they respect existing KMS-encryption controls.

Migration:
- rollout plan: enable shingling behind a feature-flag; start with internal clusters and a small set of customers; monitor object growth and GC effectiveness.
- migration helper: a one-off migrator to pre-shingle the last N model versions for customers who opt-in.

Checklist:
- [x] Shingle encoder + tests
- [x] ShingleStore S3 impl + GC hooks
- [x] CLI flags and dry-run
- [x] Release sampler + summarizer
- [x] Unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Docs and upgrade notes
- [x] Basic perf benchmarks

How to review: focus on shingle encoding invariants (stable fingerprints across runs), GC safety (refcount correctness), and release-extract prioritization heuristics (are we omitting important regressions?).

Relevant Linear tickets: ENG-1742 (shingled metadata RFC) and ENG-1793 (concise changelog UX). Related work: this complements but does not replace the compact-profile delta machinery in profile-diff; the goal is orthogonal (store-time dedupe + human-friendly extracts).

- registry/storage: add ShingleStore abstraction and an S3-backed implementation with reference-counted shard lifecycle and garbage-collection hooks. The ShingleStore exposes a small API used by the onboard pipeline to dedupe repeated payloads across models and versions.
- release/extract: add size_aware_changelog_sampler which reads profile deltas and picks representative metric changes with heuristics tuned to shard-level diffs. The sampler produces a prioritized list of up to N blurbs (configurable) and simulates a human-friendly sentence for each blurb.
- release/cli: add release-extract --summarize flag that creates a concise release_notes.json alongside the full changelog bundle.
- tests: unit tests for shingle_encoder edge cases, integration for full onboard flow (small model and large model with repeated metadata), and e2e test for release-extract summarization.
- docs: onboarding.md adds a section on shingling and guidance for when to enable it (enterprise customers with very large provenance).

Backwards compatibility: the shingled upload falls back to a legacy full-payload path when the registry is in compatibility mode; the manifest contains a version tag so older registries ignore shards until migrated. ShingleStore provides a compatibility loader that can rehydrate full metadata for older code paths.

Performance notes: local benchmarks included in this PR show ~3x reduction in upload bytes (median) for large profiles and a 40-60% reduction in changelog size for profiles with repetitive provenance entries. These numbers vary by profile shape; the sampler is conservative by default.

Security and cost: storing shingles increases object count in cloud storage; we added a GC policy and reference counting to avoid unbounded growth. Shingles are content-addressed and immutable; they respect existing KMS-encryption controls.

Migration:
- rollout plan: enable shingling behind a feature-flag; start with internal clusters and a small set of customers; monitor object growth and GC effectiveness.
- migration helper: a one-off migrator to pre-shingle the last N model versions for customers who opt-in.

Checklist:
- [x] Shingle encoder + tests
- [x] ShingleStore S3 impl + GC hooks
- [x] CLI flags and dry-run
- [x] Release sampler + summarizer
- [x] Unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Docs and upgrade notes
- [x] Basic perf benchmarks

How to review: focus on shingle encoding invariants (stable fingerprints across runs), GC safety (refcount correctness), and release-extract prioritization heuristics (are we omitting important regressions?).

Relevant Linear tickets: ENG-1742 (shingled metadata RFC) and ENG-1793 (concise changelog UX). Related work: this complements but does not replace the compact-profile delta machinery in profile-diff; the goal is orthogonal (store-time dedupe + human-friendly extracts).

title: Introduce workload-conditioned profile compositor and changelog preview generator
preview:
Motivation: model onboarding currently produces multiple performance profiles from different probes (latency, token throughput, memory watermark) and from targeted synthetic workloads, but downstream consumers (serving, cost estimator, release notes) need a single coherent profile per variant. Manual merging is error-prone and hides important workload-dependent behavior. This PR adds a workload-conditioned profile compositor that deterministically merges multiple probe-derived profiles into a set of workload-conditioned variants (chat, summarization, long-context, embedding-heavy). It also introduces a changelog preview generator that synthesizes concise release notes describing the net change introduced by a new composite profile relative to the last released profile. The generated changelog is intended as a review artifact for curation and release ops.

What this change does: implements a compositing pipeline that (1) ingests probe profiles and workload histograms, (2) assigns workload buckets using a small configurable taxonomy, (3) computes weighted aggregates of latency/throughput percentiles and token-cost projections, (4) emits provenance metadata (input probes, seed hash, aggregation weights) and stability scores, and (5) writes composite profiles into the registry. The pipeline runs as a staged job in onboard-worker with safe idempotency keys. The changelog previewer computes deltas (absolute and relative), highlights potential regressions for the top-3 customer workload buckets, and produces a human-readable preview snippet saved alongside the profile draft.

Backward compatibility and opt-out: composition is opt-in via the onboard manifest field "compose_profiles: true". If absent, onboarding preserves current behavior. We added a global feature flag (registry config) to enable automatic composition for accounts that want it.

Testing and validation: unit tests for the aggregator, property tests to ensure deterministic results for identical inputs, integration tests that run small synthetic workloads through the full ingest->compose->publish flow, and an e2e smoke test that exercises changelog preview generation. CI initially exposed a flaky fixture in the ranking comparator; tests were adjusted to be time-insensitive and the CI run was re-triggered.

Rollout and migration notes: composite profiles are created as new variant objects (suffix "-composed"), not by mutating existing variants. This enables safe rollout and easy rollback. A follow-up task is tracked in ENG-11789 to add an automatic canary that will route 10% of traffic to the composed variant for a week before full promotion.

Related work: builds on earlier probe ingestion work (ENG-11234) and complements the release note automation pipeline.

Summary: adds workload-conditioned composition, deterministic provenance sealing, changelog preview generation, feature flag, tests, and docs for operators.

title: Add onboard-priority heuristics and contextual changelog extractor
preview:
Context and motivation: As the intake stream grows, reviewers and curators are overwhelmed by raw onboard artifacts. This PR introduces a priority-scoring heuristic and an automated, context-aware changelog extractor to make onboarding reviews and release notes generation actionable and concise. The heuristic combines telemetry (sampled latency/throughput), footprint (size, quantization), legal signals (license, embargo), and delta impact (perf regressions vs baseline) into a single probabilistic priority score. The changelog extractor uses metadata diffs, curated prompt-set examples, and example-based summarization rules to produce one-line and paragraph-level release snippets suitable for downstream release automation. Key changes: 1) New pipeline: services/onboard/priority_scorer.py (probabilistic ranker, supports pluggable feature transforms) 2) Migration: db/migrations/20251103_add_priority_scores.sql (adds model_priority_score float, priority_reason text) 3) Changelog extractor: services/onboard/contextual_changelog.py (rules, sample extraction, redact hooks) 4) CLI: tools/onboard-cli add `rank-models` and `preview-changelog` commands to allow fast local curation 5) Admin UI hooks: ui/registry/priority_flag.tsx (opt-in curation pane) 6) Tests and fixtures: tests/onboard/test_priority_scorer.py and tests/onboard/test_changelog_extractor.py 7) Docs: docs/onboard/prioritization.md and docs/onboard/changelog-format.md Implementation notes and tradeoffs:
- Score is probabilistic and intentionally conservative: we apply a sigmoid-scaled ensemble of features so scores stay interpretable across workload changes.
- To avoid false positives for legacy uploads we included a migration TTL and an opt-out metadata tag (metadata.priority_opt_out).
- Changelog extractor emits three formats: short blurb, detailed paragraph, and a compact semantic diff used by release automation.
- Redaction hooks were added to remove PII and large prompt artifacts; these can be extended by enterprise rules.
Checklist:
- [x] Priority scorer implementation
- [x] DB migration and rollback script
- [x] Changelog extractor rules + redaction
- [x] CLI end-to-end commands
- [x] Unit tests for core scoring transforms
- [x] Integration test (local) that runs rank->preview-changelog flow
- [x] Docs and changelog templates
- [x] Admin UI minimal controls
Testing and CI: Initial CI run revealed a flaky test in tests/onboard/test_changelog_extractor.py (timing-sensitive sample replay). I quarantined the test by stabilizing sample fixtures and adding a deterministic RNG seed; rerun passed. CI matrix: linux/py39 unit, integration (local sqlite), linters. Commits summary: [c1f9d2] introduce priority scorer and feature transforms [d2b7e1] add db migration and migration test [e3a4bf] implement contextual changelog extractor and redact hooks [f4b5c6] CLI commands and docs [a7c8dd] admin UI curation pane and wiring [b9e0f1] tests + flakiness fixes High-level files changed: services/onboard/priority_scorer.py services/onboard/contextual_changelog.py tools/onboard_cli.py db/migrations/20251103_add_priority_scores.sql ui/registry/priority_flag.tsx docs/onboard/prioritization.md tests/onboard/test_priority_scorer.py tests/onboard/test_changelog_extractor.py src/registry/serializers.py (small adapter), README additions Review conversation snapshot: "Priya Natarajan: Nice work, this will help triage. Two asks: (1) add a short note in the docs about how scores should be interpreted by SREs; (2) include an integration test that runs the migration and a rollback." "Maria Chen: Thanks—added SRE doc section and a migration rollback test in the latest commit. The rollback test uses a test-only fixture and cleans up inserted rows." "Evan Thompson: Can the scorer be extended to accept per-customer weighting? We should avoid global thresholds where customers' usage differs." "Maria Chen: The scorer accepts a 'weight_map' optional param; CLI passes a default weight_map but admin UI will allow overriding on a per-namespace basis in follow-up (ENG-1234)." "Liam O'Connor: One nit on the changelog extractor: it currently uses a max token cutoff of 512 for sample aggregation—might hide larger examples. Please add a link to stored sample artifacts instead of trimming." "Maria Chen: Updated extractor to emit a storage link when sample truncated; the link uses the same signed-URL mechanism as telemetry snapshots." "Priya Natarajan: Approving pending CI green." Approvals: "Priya Natarajan: approved" "Evan Thompson: approved" CI timeline: "2025-11-04: CI run failed (flaky sample replay)." "2025-11-05: Quarantine commit pushed, CI passed." Merge notes: Squash-merged on 2025-11-18 by Maria Chen; commit message includes ENG-1229 and a concise summary for release notes. Rollout plan:
- Step 1: Deploy scorer service to staging with migration in read-only mode for 48h
- Step 2: Run background scoring batch, validate distribution with curated sample set
- Step 3: Enable admin UI curation for selected reviewers
- Step 4: Unblock migration to write mode and enable changelog autosynth for low-priority models only
- Step 5: Monitor for false positives and adjust weight_map via admin flags
Observability and metrics added:
- onboard.priority.score_histogram
- onboard.changelog.extract_time_ms
- onboard.migration.rows_migrated_count
Known limitations and follow-ups:
- Need per-org default weight profiles (ENG-1234)
- More robust PII redaction for edge-case prompt formats (SEC-501)
- Perf optimization: scorer currently calculates a full feature vector for every model; we will add cached lightweight prefilters in a follow-up (perf ticket ENG-1270)

title: Add weighted probe sampler and autogen performance hint annotations
preview:
Summary: Introduces a weighted probe-sampling utility and an optional 'performance_hints' metadata annotation for model profiles. The sampler produces small, representative workload slices used by the registry to auto-generate suggested batching/quantization profiles and to estimate cost/latency impact for release notes.
Motivation: onboarding new models currently relies on heavy full-workload profiling or manual hinting. This change reduces friction by (1) providing a lightweight sampled footprint for quick calibrations, and (2) surfacing automated performance hints that can be included in changelogs and the model catalog.
Context and linked work: implements parts of ENG-1428 (deploy-time perf hint generation) and ties back to SUP-7241 (customer report: slow cold-starts for large decoder-only models).
Design notes:
- Weighted sampling: we sample examples from a model's telemetry or synthetic probe corpus with weights derived from token-length distribution, presence of long-context markers, and a novelty score (n-gram surprise). The output is a compact 'probe-seed' tarball (gzip jsonl) that the onboarding pipeline consumes.
- Performance hints: a small metadata map added to profile.yaml containing keys: cpu_estimate_ms, gpu_estimate_ms, warm_kv_cache_recommendation, suggested_batch_sizes, quantization_safety. Hints are intentionally conservative and accompanied by provenance attributes.
- Changelog automation: onboarding pipeline now offers an optional step to generate a short 'perf blurb' based on the hints. This blurb is attached to the release draft and can be edited by maintainers.
Checklist:
- [x] Add weighted sampler implementation and unit tests
- [x] Add profile annotation schema and schema migration path
- [x] Integrate sampler output into onboarding pipeline (dry-run)
- [x] Add small e2e that exercises perf blurb generation
- [x] Update registry docs and example onboarding snippet
- [x] Manual QA on 3 model families (decoder-only, encoder-decoder, embed-only)
Commits (summary):
- feat(sampler): introduce WeightedProbeSampler + sampling heuristics
- feat(onboard): consume probe-seed and emit performance_hints in profile.yaml
- chore(schema): bump profile schema v2.4 and add migration script
- test(e2e): add lightweight e2e for perf blurb generation
- docs: add onboarding example and admin guide
Notes on safety and accuracy: performance_hints are advisory; precise latency/cost remains the responsibility of the serving runtime. Hints include a provenance tag (sampler: v1, telemetry_window: 72h) and a confidence score.
Migration strategy: profile schema bumped to 2.4. Existing profiles without hints are left unchanged; the on-demand migration tool will produce probe-seeds for historical models when requested.
Files changed (high level):
- registry/sampler/weighted_sampler.py (core algorithm, 220 lines)
- registry/sampler/heuristics.py (scoring and weighting helpers)
- registry/onboard/consume_probe.py (ingest probe-seed and merge hints)
- registry/schema/profile_v2_4.yaml (schema diff + migration notes)
- tests/sampler/test_weighted_sampler.py (unit tests)
- tests/onboard/test_perf_blurb.py (integration smoke tests)
- docs/onboarding/perf-hints.md (docs and sample release blurb)
CI: initial run contained a flaky e2e on arm runners; fixed by adding a per-run seed and re-running tests. All checks green on final run.
Backwards compatibility: no breaking changes to existing APIs; schema migration is additive.
Release notes: see "release_notes" field.
Related PRs: reference for design review: pr-214030 (hardware-affinity sharding) and pr-51782 (autodraft impact statements)
Review conversation (high level):
- Diego: asked for clarification on the novelty scoring and requested a threshold to avoid sampling only long outliers. Response: added an adjustable clip parameter with sane default (0.95 percentile) and unit tests.
- Marta: requested extra provenance metadata; response: added telemetry_window and sampler_version fields and documented them.
- Leo: asked for guardrails on perf blurb wording to avoid committing inaccurate claims; response: blurb generation is templated and includes confidence qualifiers; maintainers can edit draft before publishing.
Merge: squash merge to main after approvals and passing CI.

title: Version claim notarizer and micro-benchmark replay harness
preview:
Problem: Onboarding teams were seeing transient mismatches between declared model version metadata and the performance stamps we publish. That led to confusion when a variant rolled with differing microbenchmark characteristics, which in turn caused rollout regressions. This PR introduces two complementary subsystems: a version-claim notarizer that enforces deterministic, signed version claims for model uploads and a micro-benchmark replay harness that deterministically replays a curated micro-benchmark suite to generate reproducible performance stamps.

Design summary: 1) Version claim notarizer: a lightweight preflight hook that validates incoming model metadata, computes a canonical claim (content hash + schema-normalized metadata), attaches a notarization token, and writes a provenance entry to the registry ledger. The notarizer supports optional KMS signing for enterprise Private installs. 2) Micro-benchmark replay harness: a small, deterministic runner that executes a compressed suite of microbenchmarks (latency hot paths, KV cache warmup, small/long context runs) in a sandboxed, seeded environment and outputs a compact performance stamp. The harness can be run as part of CI, on intake workers, or manually by release engineers.

What changed (high level): - New API endpoints: POST /v1/models/:id/claim and POST /v1/models/:id/replay-bench - Registry schema extension: model_provenance.claim_token and model_provenance.perf_stamp_v2 - New CLI subcommands: redwood-registry claim-notarize, redwood-registry bench-replay - Worker job: bench-replay-worker to schedule deterministic microbench runs and persist stamps - KMS integration hook under config/kms_signer (optional)

Commits (summary): [ci] add bench replay CI job; scaffold harness runner; schema: add perf_stamp_v2 and claim_token; implement notarizer preflight validation; add KMS signer interface + tests; integrate worker queue and publish hook; docs: add notarizer and bench-replay guide; tests: coverage for canonicalization and deterministic replay

Files changed (high level): src/registry/api/claims.py, src/registry/workers/bench_replay.py, src/registry/models/provenance.sql, tools/benchmarks/micro_suite/*.py, cli/redwood_registry/claim.py, docs/registry/notarizer.md, tests/test_claims.py, tests/test_bench_replay.py

Compatibility and rollout: The schema change is additive (perf_stamp_v2) and we default to falling back to the previous perf_stamp when absent. Notarizer is opt-in for existing ingestion flows; we enable it for the staged intake pipeline immediately behind a feature flag. KMS signing is gated behind config and not enabled in hosted by default.

Testing: Unit tests for canonicalization, round-trip signature verification, and deterministic benchmark outputs. End-to-end smoke: ingest a synthetic model tarball, notarize claim, enqueue bench replay, and assert persisted perf stamp. CI run required a fixed docker image with deterministic RNG seeds.

Why this is different: Previous PRs focused on release note generation or sample-based profiling. This change ties provenance (signed version claims) directly to a deterministic microbenchmark snapshot, enabling reliable compatibility assertions and automated, auditable changelog snippets derived from numeric perf diffs.

Rollout plan: 1) Enable feature flag for intake-staging; 2) Run bench-replay on historical models to backfill perf_stamp_v2 for a subset; 3) Monitor perf drift alerts and adjust micro-suite; 4) Gradually enable notarizer as the default for new uploads after 2 weeks of staging validation.

Problem: Onboarding teams were seeing transient mismatches between declared model version metadata and the performance stamps we publish. That led to confusion when a variant rolled with differing microbenchmark characteristics, which in turn caused rollout regressions. This PR introduces two complementary subsystems: a version-claim notarizer that enforces deterministic, signed version claims for model uploads and a micro-benchmark replay harness that deterministically replays a curated micro-benchmark suite to generate reproducible performance stamps.

Design summary: 1) Version claim notarizer: a lightweight preflight hook that validates incoming model metadata, computes a canonical claim (content hash + schema-normalized metadata), attaches a notarization token, and writes a provenance entry to the registry ledger. The notarizer supports optional KMS signing for enterprise Private installs. 2) Micro-benchmark replay harness: a small, deterministic runner that executes a compressed suite of microbenchmarks (latency hot paths, KV cache warmup, small/long context runs) in a sandboxed, seeded environment and outputs a compact performance stamp. The harness can be run as part of CI, on intake workers, or manually by release engineers.

What changed (high level): - New API endpoints: POST /v1/models/:id/claim and POST /v1/models/:id/replay-bench - Registry schema extension: model_provenance.claim_token and model_provenance.perf_stamp_v2 - New CLI subcommands: redwood-registry claim-notarize, redwood-registry bench-replay - Worker job: bench-replay-worker to schedule deterministic microbench runs and persist stamps - KMS integration hook under config/kms_signer (optional)

Commits (summary): [ci] add bench replay CI job; scaffold harness runner; schema: add perf_stamp_v2 and claim_token; implement notarizer preflight validation; add KMS signer interface + tests; integrate worker queue and publish hook; docs: add notarizer and bench-replay guide; tests: coverage for canonicalization and deterministic replay

Files changed (high level): src/registry/api/claims.py, src/registry/workers/bench_replay.py, src/registry/models/provenance.sql, tools/benchmarks/micro_suite/*.py, cli/redwood_registry/claim.py, docs/registry/notarizer.md, tests/test_claims.py, tests/test_bench_replay.py

Compatibility and rollout: The schema change is additive (perf_stamp_v2) and we default to falling back to the previous perf_stamp when absent. Notarizer is opt-in for existing ingestion flows; we enable it for the staged intake pipeline immediately behind a feature flag. KMS signing is gated behind config and not enabled in hosted by default.

Testing: Unit tests for canonicalization, round-trip signature verification, and deterministic benchmark outputs. End-to-end smoke: ingest a synthetic model tarball, notarize claim, enqueue bench replay, and assert persisted perf stamp. CI run required a fixed docker image with deterministic RNG seeds.

Why this is different: Previous PRs focused on release note generation or sample-based profiling. This change ties provenance (signed version claims) directly to a deterministic microbenchmark snapshot, enabling reliable compatibility assertions and automated, auditable changelog snippets derived from numeric perf diffs.

Rollout plan: 1) Enable feature flag for intake-staging; 2) Run bench-replay on historical models to backfill perf_stamp_v2 for a subset; 3) Monitor perf drift alerts and adjust micro-suite; 4) Gradually enable notarizer as the default for new uploads after 2 weeks of staging validation.

title: Introduce declarative release annotations and runtime profile hinting
preview:
Context and motivation:
We frequently onboard new models with performance guidance buried in PR bodies or internal wiki pages. That makes it hard for the serving runtime and release automation to pick optimal defaults (quantization, batch sizing, routing policy) and for release notes tools to produce consistent changelogs. This PR introduces a small, intentionally-declarative annotation layer on model release manifests and ties those annotations into the changelog generator and onboarding checks.

What this PR does (high-level):
1) Adds a new optional top-level 'release_annotations' block to model manifest schema. The block is expressive but constrained: keys include "recommended_quant", "latency_ms_p99", "preferred_batching" (list), "compat_notes", and "release_reason".
2) Implements a parser and validator (registry/annotations/validator.py) that enforces allowed keys, ranges (e.g., latency_ms_p99 > 0), and cross-field constraints (e.g., preferred_batching values must be one of ["single", "small", "large"]).
3) Extends the changelog assembler to consume release_annotations and produce a structured release blurb that highlights runtime hints and a concise change summary. The assembler now emits a short 'runtime_hints' section in the generated changelog JSON and prose.
4) Adds a migration utility (tools/migrations/backfill_release_annotations.py) that scans existing manifests and emits a report + patch bundle with conservative default hints derived from existing performance profiles when available.
5) Connects annotations to the release gating pipeline: models with annotations that declare latency_ms_p99 will have additional automated smoke tests configured during CI to validate expected 95/99 latency under a representative micro-benchmark.
6) Adds docs and examples (docs/onboard/release-annotations.md) and CLI support (cli/modelctl annotate-release --file ...) for maintainers to add or update annotations.

Design notes and tradeoffs:
- Annotations are optional and backwards compatible. No manifest without annotations will be rejected. The migration tool encourages progressive adoption.
- Values are intentionally conservative; e.g., default recommended_quant is 'q4_0' where no fine-grained profiling exists. Teams with tighter SLOs can override.
- We avoid trying to express every runtime knob in the manifest to keep the contract stable. The annotation surface focuses on small, high-value hints that both serving and release automation can consume.

Commits (summary):
- 7f2d9b2: add schema extension and parser for release_annotations
- 02b4e6c: implement validator and unit tests for constraints and error messages
- 3a9d4c1: integrate annotations into changelog assembler and prose formatter
- bc12fda: migration tool to backfill annotations from existing profiles; dry-run mode
- f0e1a77: add CLI commands and docs; examples and e2e smoke tests
- e8c3b44: hook CI smoke test step to require latency check if latency_ms_p99 declared

Changed files (high level):
- registry/schema/model_manifest_v2.yaml (schema change)
- registry/annotations/validator.py (new)
- registry/annotations/parser.py (new)
- tooling/changelog/assembler.py (update to consume annotations)
- tools/migrations/backfill_release_annotations.py (new migration)
- cli/modelctl/commands.py (annotate subcommand)
- tests/annotations/test_validator.py (unit tests)
- tests/e2e/test_release_annotation_smoke.py (CI smoke test harness)
- docs/onboard/release-annotations.md (docs)
- examples/onboard/sample-model-with-annotations.yaml (example manifest)

Review conversation (high-level):
- Marcus Patel (review): "Like the direction. Two asks: (1) ensure validator failure messages are actionable (line+example), (2) add a note about how runtime will treat unknown keys."
- Ava Chen (author): "Addressed: validator messages now include manifest path/line where available and validator ignores unknown keys with a warning (configurable). Updated tests to exercise unknown-key behavior."
- Lucia García (review): "Changelog output looks good; can we make the prose a single sentence if the runtime_hints set is small? Also, add a migration dry-run report format so SRE can audit changes before apply."
- Ava Chen: "Prose single-sentence collapse implemented; migration tool now emits dry-run JSON report and a human-readable patch summary. Added docs link in PR body."
- Daniel Kim (approve): "Verified e2e smoke test passes on my local run and CI. Approving."

CI and checks:
- Unit tests: pass (all annotations tests green)
- Lint: pass
- Changelog assembler integration test: pass
- E2E smoke (latency check): pass on CI; note: two flaky runs previously due to infra noise, resolved by pinning micro-benchmark seed and increasing sampling window.

Migration and rollout plan:
- Step 1 (dry-run): run tools/migrations/backfill_release_annotations.py --dry-run across the registry, produce a review bundle and post to Slack channel #model-onboard for sign-off.
- Step 2 (staged apply): apply patches for low-risk models (models with existing perf profiles) and validate with CI smoke tests.
- Step 3 (full apply): after one week of signals and follow-ups, apply remaining conservative defaults and notify teams to refine annotations.

Follow-ups and TODOs (tracked as small follow-up tickets):
- Add an API endpoint for the console to render runtime_hints on model pages. (ENG-4794)
- Explore auto-suggesting preferred_batching based on historical traffic patterns.
- Consider mapping release annotations to automatic routing policies for Dedicated customers in a follow-up PR.

Why this is different from prior work:
Several prior PRs touched changelog assembly or profile stamping; this change centralizes runtime-relevant hints in a compact, auditable manifest extension and ties those hints directly to both release blurb generation and CI smoke tests. It reduces ad-hoc documentation and makes serving/runtime choices more discoverable and programmatic.

QA checklist (in PR body):
- [x] Schema changes validated with unit tests
- [x] Backfill migration dry-run produces expected patches
- [x] Changelog prose includes runtime_hints when present
- [x] E2E smoke latency checks pass when latency_ms_p99 declared
- [x] Docs updated and example manifest added

Merge outcome:
Merged by squash on 2025-11-12 after approvals and green CI. Rollout started with a dry-run and first staged apply to models with existing performance profiles.

title: Migrate model records to metadata v1 (backfill + rollback plan)
preview: Context: As part of the model metadata schema v1 rollout (see pr-18421), we need to migrate existing model/variant records so the registry can serve metadata_v1 consistently and unblock changelog generation + Console change history. Today, key fields are split across legacy columns and ad-hoc JSON; some records are incomplete and some are deployment-specific. This PR introduces a forward-only schema migration, a backfill job, and an explicit rollback procedure that preserves read-compatibility during the rollout window. Summary of changes: 1) DB schema: add metadata_v1 columns (jsonb) for model_versions and model_variants, plus schema_hash and migration bookkeeping (backfilled_at, backfill_source, backfill_warnings). Add partial indexes for common queries (model_id + version, availability filters). 2) Backfill job: new admin command `registry-admin backfill-metadata-v1` that transforms legacy fields into metadata v1 shape using the v1 schema library. Supports dry-run, batching, concurrency=1 by default, and idempotency (safe to re-run). Emits structured logs and counters per phase. 3) Safety checks: the backfill performs validation against the v1 JSON Schema, with strict vs permissive modes. Strict mode fails the run; permissive mode writes metadata_v1 plus warning annotations and a reason code. Includes a blocklist for known-bad legacy records (write no-op + emit alert) so we don’t half-migrate. 4) Read path compatibility: the registry read API continues to serve legacy fields, and adds `metadata_v1` on the response when present. No consumers are switched in this PR. A feature flag `MODEL_METADATA_V1_READ_PREFERRED` (default false) is included for staging-only verification. 5) Rollback plan: documented steps to revert reads to legacy-only, and to remove/ignore partially written metadata_v1 safely. Because the migration is additive, rollback is primarily a configuration rollback; the backfill writes can remain without impacting existing callers. Implementation details: Mapping logic is in `internal/metadata/migrate/legacy_to_v1.ts` and is intentionally conservative: (a) version: derives `version.semver` if present; otherwise sets `version.scheme=registry-seq` and preserves the legacy version string in `internal.legacy_version`. (b) tokenizer/runtime: best-effort inference from existing model_family + engine fields; if unknown, sets `compatibility.requires_human_review=true` and adds a warning. (c) availability: populates Hosted/Dedicated/Private availability based on legacy deployment flags and region allowlists; defaults to "unknown" rather than assuming global availability. (d) deprecations: carries forward existing deprecation_date fields; if only a free-form note exists, it is placed under internal.deprecation_context and not made public. Metrics/monitoring added: `model_registry.metadata_v1.backfill.records_total` (tagged by entity=model_version|variant, result=written|skipped|failed), `model_registry.metadata_v1.backfill.validation_failures_total` (tagged by reason), `model_registry.metadata_v1.backfill.duration_ms` (per batch), and `model_registry.metadata_v1.backfill.warnings_total` (tagged by warning_code). Also adds a one-time gauge report in logs at end-of-run: counts by model_family and deployment mode to help spot skew. Ops runbook (included in this PR): - Recommended rollout sequence: (1) deploy additive schema; (2) run backfill in staging with dry-run + strict mode; (3) run staging with strict write; (4) enable `MODEL_METADATA_V1_READ_PREFERRED` in staging and compare API diffs; (5) run production backfill in permissive mode with alerting; (6) follow up on warnings and re-run strict mode for clean-up. - Batch sizing guidance: start with 500 records/batch, pause between batches if DB CPU > 65% for 5m. - Alert thresholds: if failed > 0.5% in any 10m window or validation_failures spikes, page. - Success criteria: 99.9% records written or intentionally skipped with known warning codes; 0 schema validation failures in strict mode after remediation. Rollback steps (documented under `docs/ops/metadata-v1-migration.md`): 1) Disable `MODEL_METADATA_V1_READ_PREFERRED` everywhere (or confirm it remains false). 2) Stop/rollback any downstream consumers that switched to metadata_v1 reads (none in this PR). 3) If the backfill is causing DB load, stop the job; already-written metadata_v1 is safe to keep. 4) If we must revert writes, run `registry-admin backfill-metadata-v1 --revert` which clears metadata_v1 only for records written by this job (checks backfill_source and backfilled_at window), leaving any manual edits intact. 5) Post-rollback: export warning/failure report to Slack #eng-releases and attach to ENG-9822 for follow-up. Testing: Adds unit tests for legacy->v1 mapping for common model families and known edge cases (missing tokenizer, non-semver versions, quantization variants). Adds an integration test that runs the backfill against a seeded test DB and verifies idempotency and performance (bounded queries, no full table scans). Includes a regression test for the case raised in SUP-28429 (need exact change history later) by ensuring we preserve legacy identifiers in internal fields for traceability. Notes for reviewers: - The goal here is correctness + safety, not perfect metadata completeness. We prefer warnings over guessing. - Please focus on: (a) DB migration safety (locks/index builds), (b) idempotency + revert correctness, and (c) whether warning codes are actionable for follow-up. - Connor: release train implications are in the runbook; this should land before we turn on CI schema-diff checks in other repos.

title: introduce-model-accession-canonicalization-and-release-tape
preview:
Motivation: onboarding produced multiple informal identifiers for the same model (aliases, git tags, local upload ids). That ambiguity propagates into changelogs, rollback tooling, and audit exports. This PR introduces a compact canonical 'Model Accession' (MA) assigned at onboarding and a small, signed 'release tape' artifact that bundles the accession, profile snapshot, and a provenance hash for release-note automation.

What this PR does: 1) Adds an accession generator and persistence layer: a short, globally-unique accession (MA-YYYY-<base32>) recorded on model create. 2) Adds a release-tape assembler (lightweight JSONL artifact) that contains the accession, model metadata canonicalized, performance-profile snapshot, and a provenance hash signed by the registry key. 3) Adds 'profile guards' to validate incoming performance profiles against accountable thresholds (latency P95, mem footprint, token-cost estimate). Guards are pluggable and recorded on the tape. 4) Backfill/migration: a conservative backfill will assign accessions to existing models without changing their public aliases; release-tapes are generated only for new/updated onboard events during the feature-flag rollout. 5) CLI + API: exposes onboarding flag --emit-release-tape and a registry endpoint to fetch latest tape by accession. 6) Docs and decision record describing accession format, signing, and retention policy.

Compatibility and rollout: feature-flag guarded (registry.enable_accession=true). Default behavior is no-op for existing clients. Release-tape is optional; consumers can request the tape if they need authoritative changelog snippets. Backfill runs as a low-priority job, writing accessions to a new optional column so existing identifiers remain the source-of-truth for user-facing surfaces until teams opt-in.

Performance: accession generation is cheap (local deterministic stamp + random bytes), release-tape assembly occurs asynchronously during onboarding to avoid slowing hot paths; initial performance tests show onboarding CPU walltime +3% and no noticeable change to cold-start latency.

Security and provenance: the tape is signed with the registry's long-lived registry key; verification helpers added to the client SDKs. Signing signature and provenance hash are recorded in audit logs for compliance.

Migration plan and safety: 1) Deploy accession generator behind flag. 2) Run backfill in read-only mode to preview accession collisions (none expected). 3) Flip flag to start emitting tapes for new onboard events. 4) Run consumer integration tests for tooling relying on canonical ids. 5) Rollout to full cohort.

Testing: unit tests for accession uniqueness and determinism, integration tests for tape assembly+signature verification, e2e backfill dry-run. Also manual validation on a staging dataset of ~1.2k models.

Notes: this intentionally keeps the public alias surface unchanged. Accessions are intended to be a stable internal anchor for releases, rollbacks, and changelog automation; they are not meant to replace human-friendly names.

Related: ENG-8732 (canonical model ids), ENG-9104 (release automation).

Performance: accession generation is cheap (local deterministic stamp + random bytes), release-tape assembly occurs asynchronously during onboarding to avoid slowing hot paths; initial performance tests show onboarding CPU walltime +3% and no noticeable change to cold-start latency.

Security and provenance: the tape is signed with the registry's long-lived registry key; verification helpers added to the client SDKs. Signing signature and provenance hash are recorded in audit logs for compliance.

Migration plan and safety: 1) Deploy accession generator behind flag. 2) Run backfill in read-only mode to preview accession collisions (none expected). 3) Flip flag to start emitting tapes for new onboard events. 4) Run consumer integration tests for tooling relying on canonical ids. 5) Rollout to full cohort.

Testing: unit tests for accession uniqueness and determinism, integration tests for tape assembly+signature verification, e2e backfill dry-run. Also manual validation on a staging dataset of ~1.2k models.

Notes: this intentionally keeps the public alias surface unchanged. Accessions are intended to be a stable internal anchor for releases, rollbacks, and changelog automation; they are not meant to replace human-friendly names.

Related: ENG-8732 (canonical model ids), ENG-9104 (release automation).

title: Introduce ephemeral onboarding probe and annotated change tester
preview:
Motivation: onboarding new models currently records static profiles and full-sample performance snapshots which are heavy and slow. Teams often need a lightweight, low-cost sanity pass that validates metadata, short sequence performance, and quick compatibility checks without generating full canonical profiles. This PR adds two complementary pieces: (1) an ephemeral probe runner (epprobe) that executes short synthetic and sampled real workloads against a candidate model to produce a concise validation artifact, and (2) an annotated change tester that computes a delta against the previous release snapshot and drafts suggested changelog impact lines (latency, memory, tokenizer incompatibilities) for reviewers.

What this change does: epprobe is a small CLI + service hook that runs a configurable set of micro-benchmarks (short sequences, prefix cache warmups, tokenization sanity) and emits a signed, compact validation record stored in the onboarding bucket. The annotated change tester consumes existing model snapshots and epprobe records to compute a human-readable impact summary and a machine label (safe/regressive/unknown) which is surfaced as an auto-draft in the model release UI. The change includes: lightweight protobuf schemas for probe results, a new onboarding pipeline step, a UI draft generator, and a test harness with synthetic scenarios.

Goals/constraints: keep footprint small (no large profile uploads by default), ensure probe runs cannot access private customer data (uses anonymized or synthetic samples), make suggested changelog lines conservative and review-first (never auto-publish without human approval).

How to review: focus on the probe runner CLI, the compact protobuf schema, the delta heuristics in annotated_tester.py, and the minimal UI integration. The PR intentionally avoids changing historical profile storage paths and leaves full-profile generation opt-in.

Related: ENG-1472 adds the lightweight probe RFC; SUP-4121 is a customer request for faster onboarding feedback on new private models.

title: Introduce cohorted intake sampler and annotated release bundler
preview: Motivation: onboarding churn for large model uploads produced long, noisy changelogs that were hard for reviewers to triage. This PR implements a cohort-aware intake sampler that selects representative probes per model variant and a release bundler that packages compact annotated changelog snippets plus provenance and cohort-coverage metrics. Implementation: 1) ingest/cohort_sampler: deterministic cohort selection seeded by model manifest fingerprint; supports weighted cohorts, stratified sampling across datasets and input lengths, and optional sampling profiles passed from the CLI or CI. 2) bundler/release_bundler: consumes sampler output, computes a canonical provenance hash for the intake bundle, synthesizes concise changelog fragments prioritized by regression score and coverage delta, and emits a compact release bundle artifact (ndjson + metadata.json). 3) CLI: onboardctl additions to preview cohort composition and produce a dry-run changelog preview. 4) tests and docs: unit tests for sampler determinism and coverage heuristics, integration test for bundler end-to-end, and docs describing how to interpret cohort coverage and attach release snippets to model versions. Checklist: - [x] design doc approved (ENG-2046) - [x] sampler core implementation - [x] bundler compact changelog synthesis - [x] CLI preview and dry-run - [x] unit/integration tests - [x] docs and usage examples - [x] e2e smoke on staging Notes: deterministic behavior is important to allow reproducible diffs between releases; the sampler uses a stable hash over manifest fields to seed sampling. The bundler includes an optional verbosity flag to expand snippets for full audit. Links: linear/ENG-2046, support/SUP-842. CI: unit tests were flaky for the stratified sampler on first run due to an off-by-one in boundary math; fixed in commit 3. This PR avoids changing the public onboarding contract but adds metadata fields to release bundles; existing consumers will ignore unknown fields.
