title: introduce-cohorted-affinity-change-summarizer-and-evolution-tracker
preview:
Motivation: As teams onboard more model variants and tune performance profiles, release notes become noisy and obscure the true user-facing impact. This PR adds a cohort-aware affinity summarizer and evolution tracker which groups model metadata deltas by production cohort affinity and synthesizes concise changelog snippets for release notes and the onboarding UI.

What it does (single continuous description): Adds a background worker that computes cohort affinity vectors for models (based on observed telemetry and declared affordances), records per-profile delta hashes, and generates human-readable changelog fragments that prioritize changes likely to affect high-affinity cohorts. The change includes: DB schema migration (model_profiles.affinity_vector, changelog_fragments table), API endpoints (/v1/registries/{model}/changelog-preview), a CLI subcommand (mr changelog preview --cohort <name>), a lightweight summarization template tuned for ops/PM audiences, and a small UI hook to surface the preview in the onboarding flow.

Implementation notes: - affinity vectors are computed from existing telemetry signals (usage tags, latency buckets, token distribution) and optional manual cohort tags. We avoid PII by only using anonymized aggregated counts. - The evolution tracker stores stable delta fingerprints to avoid duplicate fragments across repeated reuploads. - Changelog generation uses a deterministic template with fallback to raw metric diffs when natural language summary scores are low. - Worker runs are idempotent and rate-limited; we added a TTL and backfill window for new registries.

Checklist: - [x] DB migration and rollback script - [x] Background worker + scheduler config - [x] API route and unit tests - [x] CLI integration tests - [x] Docs: changelog preview guide + sample outputs - [x] Small UI integration for onboarding preview (feature-flagged)

Testing and CI: Initial run failed lint (long lines in summarizer). Fixed style, added unit tests for affinity normalization and snapshot dedup. Integration test simulates two cohorts (recommendation, search) and asserts summaries rank ranking-sensitive deltas first. Load-run on the benchmark lab with 10k profiles simulated; worker completed without OOM and average worker memory under 400MB.

Performance/Cost: Worker amortizes across profiles by batching affinity computations and only materializing fragments for profiles with signal-change above a threshold. In our bench runs we reduce per-profile compute by ~72% relative to naive full-diff approach.

Backward compatibility and safety: All new columns default to nullable and migrations are reversible. Feature-flag gating for the preview endpoint and UI hook. No changes to existing model-serving paths.

Release notes: "Add cohorted affinity changelog summarizer and evolution tracker: auto-generates concise reviewable release notes tailored to cohorts; includes CLI, API, background worker, and docs."

Security/Privacy: We only use aggregated telemetry and respect KMS-protected metadata. CS team reviewed the migration for sensitive column names.

Related work: builds on the signal aggregation work (ENG-1987) and the delta-fingerprint project (ENG-2633).

Rollout plan: Roll out behind the mr-changelog-preview feature flag for internal orgs, enable backfill for active registries after one week, then expose UI preview to beta customers.

title: Synthetic probe playback and release-slate generator
preview: Motivation: We need a deterministic way to replay synthetic probe results and synthesize a candidate "release slate" (compact set of suggested release notes and risk tags) for new model variants so reviewers can triage onboarding impact without running full ingestion. This PR introduces a playback engine that replays previously captured synthetic-probe traces at configurable sample densities and a release-slate generator that produces a human-readable brief with risk indicators, sample performance deltas, and suggested changelog bullets. Design: 1) probe-playback service: accepts a probe trace id and density param, replays tokens/latency/error streams against candidate model variants in a sandbox environment, and emits condensed metrics. 2) release-slate generator: takes condensed metrics + profile metadata and produces a prioritized list of release bullets, per-route latency histograms (percentiles), quality regression flags (relative to baseline), and recommended mitigation suggestions (fallback model, quantization advice, config knobs). 3) Integration hooks: a new CLI command `reg-slate --trace <id> --variants v1,v2` and a web UI preview endpoint `/slate/preview?trace_id=...` for reviewers. Backwards compatibility: playback uses the same trace schema as existing probe exporter; no changes to stored traces required. Rollout plan: deploy probe-playback as a canary behind a feature flag; enable UI preview for internal curators first; iterate on slate wording based on reviewer feedback. Performance notes: a lightweight replay at 10% density runs ~3x faster than full ingestion and produces stable delta estimates within +/-2.5% on our sampled workloads. Security and infra: sandboxed ephemeral runs with KMS-backed credentials; traces scrub PII by the existing probe-export sanitizer before playback. Tests: unit tests for trace -> condensed metrics transformation, e2e tests for CLI and preview endpoint, and a small benchmark harness that validates density vs accuracy trade-offs. Checklist: [x] unit tests added [x] e2e CLI test added [x] feature-flag gating [x] docs stub updated. Related work: builds on earlier probe sampling and changelog summarizers but moves decisioning into a deterministic replay + slate generation flow to speed reviewer cycles.

title: Add benchmark serializer and release timeliner
preview:
Why: onboarding produces many per-variant benchmark artifacts (latency histograms, P50/P95 traces, throughput stamps). We need a compact, versioned on-disk format so the registry can store, index, and compose those artifacts into human-facing release notes and machine-checkable regression timelines. This PR introduces a small, portable 'benchmark serializer' and a 'release timeliner' service that composes micro-release entries into a chronological digest for changelogs and downgrade/rollout playbooks.

What: single PR that implements: (1) bench serializer (binary+JSON compact representation with v1 header and stable field ordering); (2) timeliner CLI and library that consumes serialized benchmark artifacts and emits a microrelease timeline (JSONL) and a condensed markdown digest for release notes; (3) CI hook to assemble timeline artifacts and attach a release digest draft to the linear ticket; (4) tests and docs.

Design notes: serializer is intentionally conservative: header = {format: "rdw.bench", version: "v1", model_id, variant, timestamp, schema_hash}. Payload stores compact arrays for histo buckets and numeric envelopes for p50/p90/p95/p99 and token-throughput samples. Strings are interned; field order is fixed to ensure deterministic hashes across languages. Timeliner groups artifacts by model and semantic version, applies heuristics to label regressions (>= 10% P95 regression and certainty heuristic based on sample counts), and emits per-release summary sentences plus a minimal YAML frontmatter so docs tooling can assemble it into release notes.

Checklist: [x] bench serializer implementation and roundtrip tests; [x] timeliner CLI + library; [x] deterministic hashing and stable ordering tests; [x] CI integration for release digest assembly; [x] docs and usage examples; [x] migration plan (no DB schema change; serializer stored as blobs under existing bench_artifacts table).

Commits summary: - 8d3f9b0: initial bench serializer and schema v1; - b4c12e1: add deterministic hashing and interning map; - c1a7f43: timeliner library + CLI + grouping heuristics; - ef2d4c9: add CI release hook and draft generator; - 3a7bce8: tests, docs, and examples.

Files touched (high-level): src/serializer/bench_serializer.py; src/timeliner/compile_timeline.py; cli/commands/timeliner.py; tests/test_bench_serializer.py; tests/test_timeliner_integration.py; docs/registry/release-timeliner.md; ci/release-timeliner-hook.yml; migrations/2025_02_add_bench_blob.sql; examples/benchmarks/sample_model_v1.bench.jsonl.

Review conversation (high level): 
- Diego: "This looks promising — two asks: ensure the serializer is language-portable (we may need a protobuf shim) and make the P95 regression threshold configurable via release policy."
- Ava: "Agree on portability. Kept v1 as a JSON-friendly compact blob; added a tiny proto shim in serializer/proto_shim.py and a schema_hash field so future versions can include protobuf encoding while keeping v1 header. Added --regression-threshold flag and policy config for timeliner."

- Priya: "Can we avoid blowing up storage? How big are typical serialized artifacts?"
- Ava: "Roundtrip tests show typical artifact ~1.2–3.4 KB once interned and compressed; CI stores gzipped blobs by default. We also added a retention hint and recommend registry retention policy for 'microrelease' artifacts (30/90/365)."

- Luca: "Please add more integration tests that simulate multiple variant arrivals and ensure timeline ordering is stable across clocks and ingestion races."
- Ava: "Added deterministic ordering using model_id+semver+timestamp and a tie-breaker of stable hash; added integration test 'test_timeliner_integration.py' that fuzzes arrival order."

CI notes: initial pipeline failed lint (black/flake8) and had a flaky test; fixed formatting and increased test timeout on CI; final run: all unit/integration tests passed, docs build succeeded, release hook validated.

Migration / Compatibility: no DB schema migrations needed. The serializer blobs are stored in the existing bench_artifacts table under new column 'artifact_blob' (added via non-disruptive migration script included but not required for storage backend that uses object store). Timeliner is opt-in: registry config key 'timeliner.enabled' defaults to false until feature roll is complete. v1 header allows future encodings; tooling will ignore unknown fields gracefully.

How to use (examples): 
- Serialize a benchmark: python -m src.serializer.bench_serializer --input samples/raw_latency.json --output sample_model_v2.bench
- Compile a timeline: redwood-registry timeliner compile --input-dir artifacts/ --output timeline-2025-02-17.jsonl --md-out release-digest-2025-02-17.md --regression-threshold 0.1

Why this is different from prior work: previous PRs focused on profile ingestion and release blurb generation in prose; this PR standardizes a compact, deterministic binary-friendly artifact format intended for long-lived storage and automated timeline assembly. It bridges low-level benchmark artifacts with user-facing release draft automation and enables machine-readable regression signals that can feed the alerting playbooks in SRE.

Merge: squash and merge after approvals. Merge message: "Add compact benchmark serializer (v1) + release timeliner; CLI, CI hook, tests, docs."

title: add-transitive-dependency-graph-and-rolling-profile-calibration-hook
preview:
Motivation: onboarding new model families has become harder to maintain when a single upstream weight change ripples through variants (quantized builds, fused kernels, etc). We need a canonical, machine-friendly representation of transitive model dependencies and an automated calibration job that re-evaluates performance profiles on a small set of canonical prompts to keep profile metadata current and to generate compact delta release notes.

What this PR does: adds a lightweight transitive-dependency graph to model metadata, implements a rolling-profile-calibration hook that runs in a low-cost sandbox, and wires the calibration output into the changelog assembler so we can publish concise, delta-based release notes for profile updates. The calibration job is conservative by design: it flags changes, produces a delta bundle, and only updates a profile stamp when thresholds are met or after human approval (configurable).

Design highlights: 1) metadata: ModelProfile now includes fields: `transitive_dependencies` (list of model ids + relation reason), `compatibility_graph_sha` (checksum for graph snapshot), and `calibration_snapshot` (timestamped probe results). 2) calibration hook: a scheduled service that loads the model variant in an isolated runner, runs a curated small corpus of prompts (opt-in per-model), measures latency/throughput/p99 and a few quality metrics (BLEU/ROUGE surrogate, token perplexity where applicable), then produces a normalized profile delta. 3) changelog integration: deltas are compacted and appended to the existing release assembler; small, safe adjustments may be auto-applied if they pass stability checks and the model owner has enabled "auto-calibrate". 4) safety & audit: every auto-change emits an audit record and a signed delta bundle; the console shows a compact human-readable blurb and the raw delta.

Compatibility & migration: existing profiles without transitive graph fields remain valid; the migration job will backfill `compatibility_graph_sha` for models that have origin metadata available. The calibration hook is opt-in by default for older models; new onboarded models get a default small probe set unless opted out.

Testing and validation: unit tests for graph serialization and checksum verification; end-to-end test harness that runs the calibration flow against a small internal model (ci-calib/test-1) to verify non-destructive behavior; load tests to ensure the sandbox runner respects resource limits and does not affect serving pools.

Operational notes: calibration job runs at low priority in the infra sandbox with default cadence of 72 hours; adjustable per-customer; rollback is supported by restoring previous profile JSON and revoking the `compatibility_graph_sha` update.

Open questions / follow-ups: 1) what gating thresholds for auto-apply are appropriate across model families (ENG-5021 is tracking an experimental policy); 2) UX in console for reviewing auto-applied deltas (docs included in this PR).

What this PR does: adds a lightweight transitive-dependency graph to model metadata, implements a rolling-profile-calibration hook that runs in a low-cost sandbox, and wires the calibration output into the changelog assembler so we can publish concise, delta-based release notes for profile updates. The calibration job is conservative by design: it flags changes, produces a delta bundle, and only updates a profile stamp when thresholds are met or after human approval (configurable).

Design highlights: 1) metadata: ModelProfile now includes fields: `transitive_dependencies` (list of model ids + relation reason), `compatibility_graph_sha` (checksum for graph snapshot), and `calibration_snapshot` (timestamped probe results). 2) calibration hook: a scheduled service that loads the model variant in an isolated runner, runs a curated small corpus of prompts (opt-in per-model), measures latency/throughput/p99 and a few quality metrics (BLEU/ROUGE surrogate, token perplexity where applicable), then produces a normalized profile delta. 3) changelog integration: deltas are compacted and appended to the existing release assembler; small, safe adjustments may be auto-applied if they pass stability checks and the model owner has enabled "auto-calibrate". 4) safety & audit: every auto-change emits an audit record and a signed delta bundle; the console shows a compact human-readable blurb and the raw delta.

Compatibility & migration: existing profiles without transitive graph fields remain valid; the migration job will backfill `compatibility_graph_sha` for models that have origin metadata available. The calibration hook is opt-in by default for older models; new onboarded models get a default small probe set unless opted out.

Testing and validation: unit tests for graph serialization and checksum verification; end-to-end test harness that runs the calibration flow against a small internal model (ci-calib/test-1) to verify non-destructive behavior; load tests to ensure the sandbox runner respects resource limits and does not affect serving pools.

Operational notes: calibration job runs at low priority in the infra sandbox with default cadence of 72 hours; adjustable per-customer; rollback is supported by restoring previous profile JSON and revoking the `compatibility_graph_sha` update.

Open questions / follow-ups: 1) what gating thresholds for auto-apply are appropriate across model families (ENG-5021 is tracking an experimental policy); 2) UX in console for reviewing auto-applied deltas (docs included in this PR).

title: Add v1 quantization profile schema, sample profiles, and registry-side validation
preview:
This PR introduces the v1 Quantization Profile schema to the model registry, adds example profile JSONs for a small set of initial model variants, and wires validation into the registry publish/update path. The goal is to ensure profiles are versionable, auditable, and safe to consume by downstream control plane/runtime code by enforcing strict constraints at the point of publication.

Summary of changes: 1) Add `quantization_profile.schema.json` (v1) and a typed validator wrapper used by the profile ingestion codepath. 2) Add sample profiles under `examples/quantization_profiles/v1/` to serve as canonical references for Applied ML authors and for CI fixtures. 3) Enforce validation rules in `RegistryProfileService.publishQuantProfile()` and `RegistryProfileService.updateQuantProfile()` with clear error messages intended to surface in API responses and internal tooling. 4) Add unit tests for schema validation, error messaging, and backward-compatible behavior when no quantization profile fields are present.

Key v1 schema fields (high-level): `profile_id` (stable), `profile_version` (semver), `model_id`, `model_variant`, `quant_method` (e.g. awq/gptq/fp8/int8), `weight_precision`, `activation_precision`, `calibration` (dataset ref + hash), `constraints` (gpu_skus, min_driver, max_context_tokens, supported_sequence_length_buckets), `artifacts` (logical names + checksums; actual blob storage and signing will land in follow-up), and `known_caveats` (explicit list for docs/console warnings).

Validation notes: we intentionally validate for determinism and safety: required fields must be present; enums are strict; semver parsing required; `model_id`/`model_variant` must correspond to an existing registry entry; `constraints.max_context_tokens` must be <= model variant max; `gpu_skus` must be from the curated SKU allowlist; and `calibration.dataset_hash` must be a SHA256 hex string. Artifact references are allowed but not required in this PR (to avoid blocking early schema adoption); if present, checksums must be SHA256.

Out of scope: artifact storage/signing (tracked separately), compatibility matrix generation, control-plane route configuration, and runtime application of the profiles.

Why now: downstream teams (Platform + Runtime) are starting integration work and need a stable schema with enforced validation so they can rely on registry reads without defensive parsing in every service. Having canonical examples also reduces churn in field naming and enables docs + SDK examples to point to a consistent format.

Risk: low. Changes are additive and gated to the new profile endpoints; no changes to existing model metadata schema. Existing model publish flows are unaffected unless a quantization profile payload is provided.

Rollout plan: land schema + validator behind existing registry feature gate `registry.quant_profiles.enabled` (default off in prod); use examples + CI tests in this PR; enable gate for internal tooling in staging once follow-up control-plane work is ready.

title: Versioned quantization profile artifact storage + signing/integrity verification
preview:
This PR adds versioned artifact storage for Quantization Profile Library profiles and enforces integrity checks (sha256 + signature verification) on fetch. Motivation: profiles are referenced from route config and must be auditable/reproducible; we need deterministic resolution to an immutable artifact, with a clear deprecation/supersedence story and guardrails against tampering/corruption in object storage.

What’s included:
- Versioned profile artifacts: profile versions now resolve to an immutable `artifact_ref` that points to a content-addressed blob plus a stable registry path.
- Manifest + checksums: each artifact upload produces a manifest with sha256 digests for each payload file and the overall bundle.
- Signing: registry signs the manifest (ed25519) using a KMS-backed key; clients verify signatures before accepting the artifact.
- Integrity verification on read path: registry service verifies sha256 + signature before serving download URLs or returning inline metadata (fail-closed).
- Deprecation fields: adds `deprecated_at`, `deprecation_reason`, and `superseded_by` to profile metadata; publish tooling rejects new references to deprecated versions (unless explicitly forced for rollback/testing).
- Backwards compatibility: existing non-versioned artifacts are supported behind a compatibility shim (read-only); any new publishes require versioned + signed artifacts.

Storage layout:
- Object store key format: `quant-profiles/{model_id}/{profile_id}/v{version}/bundle.tar.zst`
- Manifest stored alongside: `.../manifest.json` and `.../manifest.sig`
- Registry DB stores: `artifact_ref`, `sha256`, `signature`, `signing_key_id`, `created_by`, timestamps.

Threat model / guardrails:
- Prevent accidental corruption: sha256 verification on fetch.
- Prevent unauthorized mutation: signature verification + object store path immutability (write-once semantics at the app layer).
- Key rotation supported via `signing_key_id`; verification trusts a configured allowlist of keys.

Notes:
- This does not change profile schema fields related to quant parameters; it only affects artifact lifecycle and verification.
- The upload/sign path is only enabled for internal publisher tooling in this PR. Public/customer APIs remain read-only.

Testing:
- Unit: manifest generation + signature verification, key allowlist behavior, deprecation gating.
- Integration: upload -> sign -> store -> fetch -> verify -> serve URL.
- Negative tests: tampered manifest, tampered bundle, unknown key id, missing signature.

Rollout:
- Deployed behind `REGISTRY_ENABLE_SIGNED_PROFILE_ARTIFACTS` (default off in prod until publisher tooling is updated).
- Once enabled, new publishes will require signing, but existing profiles continue to resolve via legacy path.

Follow-ups:
- Wire policy engine/runtime consumers to record `artifact_ref` in traces/logs for end-to-end auditability.
- Add registry metrics for verification failures and legacy-path reads.

title: add runtime-affinity prober and changelog-hint exporter
preview:
Motivation and summary:
This PR introduces a lightweight 'runtime-affinity prober' (affinity_prober) that runs short, deterministic probes across a small battery of inference shapes (seq lengths, batch sizes, kv-cache warm/cold) and collates an annotated performance map for model variants. The primary goal is to supply the model registry with structured, human-readable hints for release notes (e.g., 'suitable for 8k tokens with quantized-llm on A100; expected median p95 latency 42ms at batch=1') and to provide downstream deploy tooling with an initial affinity signal for placement and scheduling decisions.

What this changes (high level):
- registry/probes/affinity_prober.py: new probe runner with pluggable backends (mock backend for tests + runtime adapter).
- registry/annotators/perf_hint_exporter.py: turns probe output into compact annotations and suggested one-line changelog snippets.
- onboarding/docs/perf-probe.md: walkthrough for maintainers (how to run locally and interpret results).
- tests/test_affinity_prober.py and tests/test_perf_hint_exporter.py: unit + integration mocks.
- registry/schema/affinity_annotation_v1.yaml: small schema for annotations.
- examples/onboard/sample-probe-config.yaml: sample config used by CI for smoke runs.

Description / checklist:
- [x] Implement probe runner core + backend adapter pattern
- [x] Add exporter that synthesizes short release hints and a structured annotation payload
- [x] Add schema and validation for annotation bundle
- [x] Unit tests for logic paths and CI smoke config
- [x] Documentation: onboarding doc + example config
- [x] Integration test that runs against registry's mock-runtime in CI (short, <30s)

Commits (summary):
- c1a2b3: initial affinity prober interface + mock backend
- d4e5f6: add perf hint exporter and annotation schema; wire to onboard pipeline
- a7b8c9: tests and docs; CI smoke runner and sample configs

Changed files (high level snippets):
- registry/probes/affinity_prober.py (new)
  - core class: AffinityProber(collections.namedtuple) -> run_probe() returns AnnotationBundle
  - supports probes: short_seq, long_seq, kv_warm, batch_pathology
- registry/annotators/perf_hint_exporter.py (new)
  - function: generate_release_hints(annotation) -> {blurb, tags, perf_buckets}
- registry/schema/affinity_annotation_v1.yaml (new)

title: policy-aware-onboard-reviewer-bot-and-compact-annotation-generator
preview:
Context and motivation: Onboarding new model versions frequently requires cross-checking provenance, licenses, performance baselines, and safety policy assertions. Review cycles are slowed by repetitive checklist items and by non-standardized annotation formats in PRs. This change introduces a lightweight 'onboard-reviewer-bot' service and a compact annotation schema to accelerate reviews, surface policy-level risks in human-readable language, and produce suggested rollback manifests and changelog snippets when onboarding fails checks.

Primary goals: 1) Run deterministic policy checks (license, KMS usage, dataset license tags) and lightweight performance delta checks against stored baselines. 2) Present concise, human-readable annotations in the PR description so reviewers can make decisions quickly. 3) When a critical check fails, create a suggested rollback manifest (k8s manifest fragment) and a one-line changelog blurb for release automation. 4) Fit within existing model-registry hooks and be configurable via repo-level .onboardrc.

What changed (high level): - New service: tools/onboard-bot/ (Go) with a small HTTP webhook receiver and a lightweight policy engine that runs a combination of JSON-schema linting and rule-based checks. - New validators: registry/validators/policy_checks.py (Python) to run in CI for cross-language parity. - Annotation schema: docs/onboard-annotations.md describing the compact, human-readable annotation format and severity tiers. - Rollback generator: tools/onboard-bot/rollback_generator.go generates a k8s-style manifest fragment when a critical policy or performance regression is detected. - Integration: .github/workflows/onboard-bot.yml to run bot checks for PRs labeled 'onboard'. - Tests: tests/onboard-bot/* unit tests + integration harness using a recorded fixture of model metadata.

Commits (summary): - 8f4b2c0: initial crate for onboard-bot service and HTTP webhook receiver - 1a7d3f1: add policy rule engine (license, kms, provenance) - b2d8a3e: rollback manifest generator and compact changelog blurber - c3f0d5a: Python parity validators and shared schema definitions - d4a6e70: docs for annotation schema and admin config (.onboardrc) - e1f2b20: CI workflow and test fixtures - f9a0c11: address reviewer feedback, reduce log noise and tighten timeouts

Files changed (high-level): - tools/onboard-bot/main.go (new) - tools/onboard-bot/rollback_generator.go (new) - tools/onboard-bot/policy_engine.go (new) - registry/validators/policy_checks.py (new) - registry/schemas/onboard_schema.json (new) - .github/workflows/onboard-bot.yml (new) - docs/onboard-annotations.md (new) - .onboardrc.example (new) - tests/onboard-bot/test_policy_engine.py (new) - tests/onboard-bot/fixtures/model-v1.json (new) - README.md (updated) - docs/onboarding.md (updated) - scripts/deploy_onboard_bot.sh (new) - tests/ci/integration_onboard.sh (updated)

Small illustrative snippet (annotation format example): "annotation:severity=warning;check=performance-diff;message='Throughput -18% vs baseline (p99 stable) — investigate batching config.'"

Review conversation (chronological): - Ethan Park (requested changes, 2025-01-16): "This is promising work — two asks before merge: 1) make sure the policy rules are configurable per-namespace via .onboardrc; 2) the rollback manifest needs a dry-run flag so we don't accidentally apply anything during automation. Also please add unit tests for the KMS rule."

- Maya Chen (author reply, 2025-01-16): "Thanks — added .onboardrc support and a dry-run toggle to rollback_generator.go, and tests for KMS rule in tests/onboard-bot. PTAL."

- Leah Morales (comment, 2025-01-17): "Docs look good; can we add a short section showing how the compact changelog blurb maps to release note templates? Also, consider a sample optical layout for annotations in the PR body so it is scannable."

- Maya Chen (update, 2025-01-17): "Added docs/onboard-annotations.md with mapping examples and a recommended PR body layout. Reduced annotation verbosity by collapsing non-critical findings into a single summary line."

- Diego Torres (approval, 2025-01-20): "Approved after CI stabilized. One nit: consider adding a telemetry counter for 'annotation_count' so we can monitor annotation churn over time."

- Maya Chen (follow-up, 2025-01-20): "Added basic telemetry counters (increment-only) and a TODO to expand labels in future PRs."

CI summary: - First run (2025-01-16): lint passed, unit tests: fail (flaky fixture for KMS in CI container), integration tests: pending. - Second run after fix (2025-01-17): lint passed, unit tests: pass, integration smoke: fail (timeout on container startup). - Reran with increased timeouts and fixture pin (2025-01-20): all checks passed. Final status: pass.

Security and compliance notes: - All license checks are rule-based and map to registry/schemas/onboard_schema.json; failures are high-severity by default. - Rollback manifests are output as suggestion files in tools/onboard-bot/suggestions/ and never auto-applied; the webhook requires an explicit admin action to promote suggestions. - No secret material is stored by the bot; KMS rules only validate that a key reference exists and matches allowed patterns.

Backward-compatibility: - The annotation format is additive and non-breaking; existing onboarding flows will ignore new annotations when not consumed. - .onboardrc is optional; default organization-level policy applies when missing.

Operational notes and rollout plan: 1) Deploy webhook receiver to staging (k8s helm chart not included in this PR) and enable via .github labels on a small subset of model registry PRs. 2) Observe annotation_count and false-positive rate for two weeks. 3) Iterate on rule thresholds and add more performance checks (latency percentiles) in follow-ups.

Merge outcome: Merged via squash on 2025-01-22 after CI green and approvals from Diego and Leah. Changelog auto-generated snippet stored in registry/onboard-releases/2025-01-22.md for release automation.

Related work: ENG-4781 (policy-driven onboarding), ENG-4990 (changelog automation), SUP-882 (support escalation for KMS edge-cases).

What changed (high level): - New service: tools/onboard-bot/ (Go) with a small HTTP webhook receiver and a lightweight policy engine that runs a combination of JSON-schema linting and rule-based checks. - New validators: registry/validators/policy_checks.py (Python) to run in CI for cross-language parity. - Annotation schema: docs/onboard-annotations.md describing the compact, human-readable annotation format and severity tiers. - Rollback generator: tools/onboard-bot/rollback_generator.go generates a k8s-style manifest fragment when a critical policy or performance regression is detected. - Integration: .github/workflows/onboard-bot.yml to run bot checks for PRs labeled 'onboard'. - Tests: tests/onboard-bot/* unit tests + integration harness using a recorded fixture of model metadata.

title: Add usage-aware profile stitcher and autobrief generation for onboarding
preview:
Motivation: When multiple intake profiles for the same model arrive from different partners/environments we need a deterministic, usage-weighted reconciliation so the registry surface (performance profiles, recommended quantization, changelog blurb) reflects real customer signal rather than noisy probes. This PR introduces a usage-aware 'profile stitcher' that merges incoming onboarding profiles using per-sample weights derived from telemetry and dataset provenance. It also adds an autobrief generator that drafts concise release notes summarizing changes between the active profile and the new stitched profile.

What this PR does (high level):
- Add profile stitcher implementation that: deduplicates metrics, normalizes latency/throughput histograms, applies per-field weighting, and resolves conflicting knobs (quantization/affordances) via configurable heuristics.
- Add autobrief generator that summarizes: top metric deltas, notable config drift (e.g., sequence length, batchability), recommended rollout safety notes, and suggested canary thresholds.
- Add DB migration: profile_stitch_runs table + indexing to support audit and rollback.
- Add CLI and API hooks to invoke stitching as part of onboard pipeline (on-demand and scheduled).
- Add unit/integration tests and small e2e fixture to validate summaries and determinism.
- Update onboarding docs with configuration knobs and migration notes.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation of stitcher core algorithm and config
- [x] Autobrief text templates + examples
- [x] DB migration and rollback path
- [x] Tests for deterministic output with fixed seed
- [x] Docs and example CLI usage
- [x] Performance microbench harness for stitcher (latency under typical payload sizes)

Impact and safety: The new stitcher is opt-in by default for existing models; the onboard pipeline exposes a flag `--stitch-mode=off|sampled|always`. For safety we default to `sampled` which runs stitcher asynchronously and writes a draft autobrief for manual review before rolling into the active profile. The autobrief is intentionally conservative in recommendations; full automated rollouts require an additional opt-in.

Migration notes: Run the included migration to create `profile_stitch_runs`. Existing profiles are unaffected until a stitch is executed. See docs/onboarding/usage-stitcher.md for CLI examples and the rollback procedure.

Related work: builds on ENG-3180 (profile canonicalizer) and ENG-2872 (release blurb schema).

title: Introduce probe-schema sampler and release-notes weaver
preview:
Motivation: onboarding a model currently produces large, noisy changelogs and our sampling for profile calibration is expensive and sometimes biased by a few long sequences. This PR introduces (1) a small, explicit 'probe schema' for captured onboarding probes (tokenizer id, sequence length histogram, canonical prompts, perf anchors) and (2) a sampler implementation that selects representative probe subsets by stratified length buckets + semantic clustering so downstream profiling and release-note diffs are stable and cheap. Additionally this PR adds a release-notes 'weaver' that synthesizes concise human-readable release blurbs from profile diffs and telemetry deltas (latency, token-cost, quality regression hints). The weaver outputs a compact markdown snippet and a machine-readable changelog fragment for the console UI.

What this PR changes (high level): added a probe schema and serializers; a cluster-based sampler; a release-weaver CLI and library; integration with the model-intake pipeline to emit compact changelog fragments; docs and tests. The description below is intentionally a single continuous string used as the PR body in the repo.

Checklist: - [x] Probe schema (JSON Schema + Python dataclass) and serializer/deserializer - [x] Sampler: stratified-length + embedding-cluster sampler with configurable budget - [x] Release-weaver engine: templates + delta heuristics + CLI (rgm release-weave) - [x] Small compatibility shim in intake hook to attach probe-summary to profile bundles - [x] Unit tests + integration workflow - [x] Docs for registry operators and example outputs - [x] Feature-flag gating (registry.feature.release_weaver) for gradual rollout

Why this approach: stratified-length sampling reduces bias from tail examples; embedding-based clustering keeps semantic diversity; explicit probe schema makes diffs deterministic and reduces churn in release notes. The weaver focuses on delta-first blurbs: e.g. "+12ms p95 (seq<=256), -3% token-cost via quantized variant".

Related: ENG-4278 (probe-driven onboarding), ENG-4301 (release automation), earlier telemetry work on profile stability. Links: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4278, https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4301

title: Introduce sidecar metadata augmenter and contextual release clipper
preview: Motivation: Add a lightweight "sidecar" service that augments model profiles at intake time with runtime telemetry hints, provenance snippets and prioritized impact signals, and a companion 'release clipper' that generates minimal, contextual changelog fragments automatically for model version rollouts. This reduces manual churn when onboarding models and helps signal which metadata deltas are likely to affect serving economics or quality. Design overview: (1) augmenter sidecar runs during profile ingestion and attaches: sampled latency histograms, hardware-affinity hints, embedding-dimension provenance, and a small 'culprit' tag computed by delta heuristics; (2) release clipper consumes profile metadata deltas and telemetry to emit a compact one-line changelog entry + 2-line rationale suitable for release notes and downstream consumers. Implementation highlights: new microservice written in Python using async intake, small redis cache for ephemeral affinity lookups, delta scorer library shared with registry core. Backwards compatibility: sidecar augments are additive to existing profile schema; no mandatory fields are removed. Checklist: - implement augmenter service and unit/integration tests; - wire clipper into release pipeline; - add short docs and onboarding guide; - add e2e test that covers a model intake -> augmenter -> clip generation. Related: ENG-1987 for priority sampling; SUP-4321 support ticket requested more granular changelogs. Rollout plan: feature-flag gated rollout for 2 weeks with telemetry on clipped-changelog-usage; opt-out label for customers who require pristine profile blobs. Notes: kept changelog snippets intentionally conservative to avoid noisy releases; scoring heuristics tuned to favor performance-impacting changes (kv-cache-affinity, seq-length-profile shifts, quantization metadata).

title: Introduce annotated affinity stamps and chunked changelog compressor
preview:
Motivation: onboarding high-capacity and quantized variants has become noisier in release notes and harder to map to hardware guidance for infra. This PR introduces two coordinated pieces: 1) "annotated affinity stamps" — a small, versioned metadata artifact attached to model variants that captures recommended hardware affinity (e.g. ampere-80, triton-quant-fast, int8-friendly), expected memory working set, and recommended quantization knobs; and 2) a "chunked changelog compressor" — a post-processing step that consumes model intake diffs and emits condensed release chunks (human-friendly bullets + linked deep diffs) so changelogs stay readable for large model updates. Goals: reduce support noise during rollouts, surface deterministic hardware affinity suggestions to serving/runtime teams, and keep changelog summaries concise while preserving drill-down details.

What this PR does (high-level): added a new affinity annotation schema and signer for variant manifests; an upload hook that auto-populates affinity hints based on profiling heuristics and historical run-affinity matches; a compact serializer and compressor that groups changelog hunks into prioritized "chunks" and auto-generates teaser lines for release notes; integration tests and docs; migration tool to backfill affinity stamps for recent variants.

Implementation notes: affinity stamps are stored as a small stamped JSON file in the model bundle (affinity-stamp-v1.json) and a lightweight provenance hash is appended to the variant metadata. The chunked changelog compressor runs as a transform in the release pipeline (release-assembler) and uses heuristics: group by semantic change type (perf/regression/config), weight by user-facing impact (example-driven), and produce at most 6 top-level bullets with links to expanded hunks. The compressor is deterministic given the same diff seed and seed version.

Backwards compatibility and rollout: old variants without affinity stamps continue to function — runtime falls back to default routing. The upload hook is opt-in by default; orgs can enable auto-stamping via a feature flag at intake. The migration/backfill job writes affinity stamps as a separate commit and is reversible.

Testing/validation: unit tests for serializer, end-to-end integration with release-assembler in CI, a sampled backfill run capturing 500 variants for validation, and a small canary deploy of the compressed changelog to the docs pipeline.

Doc changes: docs/onboard/affinity-stamps.md, docs/release/changelog-compressor.md and a short console UI blurb to surface the top-3 affinity suggestions in the variant detail page.

Related: refers to ENG-4232 (affinity heuristics prototype) and ENG-4301 (changelog UX revamp).

title: Introduce staged intake intent and profile shadowing for safe, measurable model rollouts
preview: Context: As model intake velocity grows we need a lightweight safe path to stage new model manifests and performance profiles without impacting live serving. This PR adds a 'staged intake intent' concept plus 'profile shadowing' so teams can register candidate model versions, run sampled traffic/probes against shadowed profiles, and automatically synthesize a preview changelog describing measured regressions and cost delta. Motivation: reduce risk of blind rollouts, make onboarding telemetry-driven, and provide concise release-ready notes for model ops. Summary of features: 1) staged intake manifest schema (staged_intake.yaml) with intent metadata (owner, canary-criteria, probe-sets, retention-ttl); 2) profile-shadow runner service that can accept a staged manifest and shadow live traffic into a sandboxed profile evaluation pipeline (local kv-cache isolation + cost telemetry); 3) probe sampler that runs a configurable subset of promptsets (promptset refs in manifest) and computes key deltas (latency P95, tokens-per-request, quality-regression score) and stores a compact report; 4) changelog synthesizer that takes probe reports and generates a draft release blurb (short, medium, long) with links to artifacts; 5) lightweight web preview in registry UI (api route /staged-preview/:id) that renders the manifest + probe deltas + proposed release snippet. Implementation notes: aim for minimal runtime impact by making shadowing opt-in per manifest and sampling at route-level; identity-preserving telemetry tags are stripped before storage to meet compliance; manifests are immutable once marked "candidate", and shadow runs are tagged with provenance (uploader, commit, manifest-hash). Backwards compatibility: existing manifest ingestion remains unchanged unless a staged intent field is present. Checklist: - [x] manifest schema and validation - [x] profile-shadow runner and sandbox isolation - [x] probe sampler + three default promptsets - [x] changelog synthesizer with templating - [x] unit + integration tests for ingestion and draft generation - [x] docs/registry/staged-intake.md draft - [x] lightweight UI preview endpoint - [x] privacy/stripper for telemetry. Risks and mitigations: memory overhead during shadow runs mitigated by limiting concurrent shadow workers and reusing KV cache objects; behavioral regressions are surfaced only in preview and do not affect routing. Next steps: allow autoscheduling of shadow runs based on traffic thresholds, add RBAC controls for who can mark a manifest candidate, and wire preview generation to the release-notes exporter.

title: Introduce intake sentinel and brief compiler for compact changelogs
preview: Context: onboarding churn and long autogenerated release notes make it hard for engineers to quickly assess risk and performance impact of new model variants. This PR adds two cooperating subsystems: an intake-sentinel that runs lightweight sanity and cost/perf heuristics during model intake, and a brief-compiler that synthesizes compact, human-friendly changelog fragments and performance highlight cards. Motivation: reduce noise in release notes, accelerate review turnaround, and catch obvious regressions earlier in the pipeline. Scope: sentinel rules (schema checks, common-freeze tokens, latency/cost delta thresholds), telemetry-sampler hook (emits sampled perf traces for new variants), brief compiler (compact prose + delta table + example prompts), automation pipeline changes to attach compiled briefs to model uploads, and tests + migration. Checklist: - implement sentinel engine and rule registry; - add telemetry-sampler integration and sampling policy; - implement brief compiler with templating and delta heuristics; - attach compiled brief to model registry artifacts and release draft; - add unit + e2e tests and CI job; - docs stub for reviewer workflow. Impact: aims to be backward-compatible with existing onboard flows; sentinel blocks uploads only when high-confidence critical violations are detected (configurable); compiled briefs are optional by feature flag. Related: ENG-2173 (onboard quality gates), ENG-2201 (changelog noise reduction).

title: Introduce lifecycle signatures and staged compatibility snippets for onboarding changelogs
preview:
Background: onboarding churn and subtle compatibility regressions have made changelogs noisy and hard to consume. We need a small, machine-readable lifecycle signal attached to model profiles so release automation can produce concise, actionable changelog snippets when a model moves between lifecycle phases (onboard -> staging -> prod). This PR introduces 'lifecycle_signature' metadata on model profiles and a staged compatibility-snippet generator that outputs human-friendly changelog fragments and suggested fallback mappings for deployments.

What this PR does: introduces a new lightweight lifecycle_signature schema (versioned), adds an onboarding hook to compute the signature, adds a changelog assembler plugin that emits staged compatibility snippets, and wires a CLI flag to preview generated snippets. The generator uses a deterministic fingerprint of (architecture, tokenizer_hash, primary_quant, perf_token_summary) and a short delta heuristic to summarize compatibility impact and performance deltas.

Checklist:
- [x] lifecycle_signature schema and migration support
- [x] onboarding hook: registry/hooks/lifecycle_signer.py
- [x] changelog assembler plugin: registry/hooks/compat_snippets.py
- [x] CLI preview: registry/cli/onboard_preview.py --staged-snippets
- [x] unit tests for signature calculation and snippet heuristics
- [x] docs: docs/onboard-lifecycle.md with examples and rollout guidance

Design notes: the lifecycle_signature is intentionally compact (hex-encoded 12 bytes + version tag) to remain stable in manifests and to avoid bloating release bundles. The staged compatibility snippet is a short paragraph and a suggested remap table (previous_alias -> recommended_variant) to help platform teams stage rollouts. The heuristics aim for conservative suggestions (only propose remaps when the signature delta + perf delta exceed thresholds).

title: Introduce onboarding contract mapper and traffic-weighted release-notes generator
preview:
Context / motivation: model onboarding churn has made release notes noisy: small metadata-only updates (typo fixes, curator comments) and large perf-affecting changes appear at the same verbosity level. Teams want concise, signal-rich changelogs that call out performance and runtime compatibility changes while suppressing low-impact edits. This PR adds two complementary pieces: (1) an onboarding contract mapper that normalizes and stamps the metadata that matters for serving/runtime compatibility, and (2) a traffic-weighted release-notes generator that scores deltas based on historical usage and staged rollout traffic to prioritize changelog sections.

What this PR does (high level):
- Introduce a lightweight onboarding_contract_mapper that: canonicalizes fields (model.aliases, quantization_profiles, runtime_hints), emits a stability fingerprint, and records a \"compatibility_contract\" object (stringified map) that downstream systems use for gating. The mapper runs in the intake path and as a pre-commit hook for model manifests.
- Add a traffic_weighted_changelog generator service that: consumes delta events and model traffic signals (ingest from telemetry snapshots), computes an impact_score that weights changes by percent-traffic and latency/cost delta, and outputs a compact release note with prioritized bullets (Performance, Runtime Compatibility, Behavioral, Metadata). The generator also groups low-impact deltas into an \"Other metadata edits\" bucket to reduce noise.
- Provide migration tooling: a one-off migrator that rehydrates existing model profiles with compatibility_contract keys and generates one sample traffic-weighted release digest for the last 30 days (stored as samples/release-digest-*.ndjson).
- Add unit + integration tests and CI job to validate contract_mapper idempotence and changelog ranking monotonicity.

Design notes and tradeoffs:
- We intentionally keep the compatibility_contract as a stable, compact string map to avoid exploding DB schema changes; the contract is designed to be backward-compatible for a two-release window.
- Impact scoring is heuristic (traffic percent * normalized perf delta + deprecation_penalty + security_flag), not ML-driven; we opted for deterministic, explainable rules in this iteration to keep auditability simple.
- The changelog generator does not automatically publish to user-facing release pages; it emits drafts for the release curator UI to review first. Future work could hook canary rollout metrics for live weighting.

Checklist:
- [x] Mapper implementation with canonicalization rules
- [x] Compatibility fingerprint + stamping
- [x] Changelog generator with traffic weighting and bucketing rules
- [x] Migrator and sample output artifacts
- [x] Unit tests for mapper and generator scoring
- [x] Integration tests with sample telemetry snapshot
- [x] CI job added and passing

- feat(onboard): add onboarding_contract_mapper and canonicalizer
- feat(changelog): implement traffic_weighted_changelog service and API
- chore(migration): add rehydrate migrator and sample output generator
- test: add unit/integration tests for mapper + changelog scoring
- ci: add changelog-scoring CI job and test fixtures

Changed areas / notable files (high level):
- src/ingest/contract_mapper.py -- core canonicalization logic and fingerprinting rules
- src/ingest/rules.yaml -- canonical field mappings and normalization rules (documented)
- src/changelog/weighting.py -- impact scoring and bucketing algorithm
- src/changelog/service.py -- API to produce draft release digests
- tools/migrators/rehydrate_compat_contract.py -- one-off migrator scaffolding
- tests/test_contract_mapper.py, tests/test_changelog_scoring.py -- test coverage for idempotence and monotonicity
- ci/workflows/changelog-scoring.yml -- new CI workflow validating scoring determinism

Review conversation (high level):
- Arielle Chen (review): \"Nice separation between contract and draft generator. Two asks: (1) make fingerprint stable across minor metadata edits, (2) add examples in rules.yaml for quant profiles.\"
- Author reply (Priya): \"Addressed: fingerprint uses a whitelist of fields and salt; added quant examples + unit tests that exercise the stability property.\"
- Mateo Alvarez (review): \"Can we avoid coupling telemetry ingestion directly in the changelog service? Suggest adding a small adapter interface so we can swap sources.\"
- Author reply: \"Added TelemetryAdapter interface and a simple in-memory adapter for the migrator; production wiring kept in ingestion config. Also added an integration test using the adapter.\"
- Jonah Beck (approval): \"Approach looks good; tests pass and migration artifacts useful for manual validation. Approving.\"

CI summary: all checks passed on merge. Notable checks:

- Added traffic-weighted release-notes generator to prioritize changelog items by inferred impact.

title: Introduce embargoed provenance ledger and microdigest generator
preview: Context: customers and internal curation teams have asked for a way to attach time-bound/embargoed metadata to model intake that is cryptographically auditable and gated for release notes. This PR adds a small provenance ledger that signs embargoed metadata, exposes an ACL-aware retrieval path, and generates a compact "microdigest" used by the Console and release automation to show concise delta-first release snippets. Motivation: reduce manual release-note drafting, centralize embargo handling for sensitive commercial or regulatory annotations, and produce deterministic microdigests for downstream QA and changelog automation. Changes: 1) API: intake endpoint accepts optional 'embargo_until' and 'embargo_reason' metadata; embargoed fields are stored encrypted and recorded in the provenance ledger with signer metadata. 2) Ledger: lightweight append-only ledger table with signer_id, signed_hash, ttl and visibility flags; signer service signs using an internal key (KMS-backed) and publishes short receipts. 3) Microdigest generator: new service that computes a compact human-friendly summary from model profile deltas (perf metrics, metadata fields, and affinity hints) and includes risk flags when embargoed fields are present. 4) UI: Console exposes a microdigest preview and marks embargoed items with an eye icon and 'until' date. 5) Migration: DB migration to add ledger table + retention TTL. Checklist: - [x] API schema and intake validation - [x] DB migration (ledger table + indices) - [x] signer service + KMS access scoped - [x] microdigest generator service + deterministic template - [x] Console preview + tests - [x] e2e: onboarding -> draft microdigest -> preview - [x] documentation: registry/embargoed-metadata.md and release-microdigest.md See Linear ENG-4821 for product context and SUP-1234 for a support customer ask. No external schema changes for downstream consumers; microdigest is opt-in during release assembly.

title: Introduce variant affordance annotator and delta-aware changelog composer
preview:
Context and motivation: We onboarded multiple model variants for the Q4 rollout and noticed reviewers and customers struggled to interpret subtle capability regressions across variants. Manual release notes were noisy and missed guidance for routing and mitigation. This PR introduces two complementary pieces: (1) a Variant Affordance Annotator that infers and stamps compact capability/constraint hints into variant metadata during intake, and (2) a Delta-aware Changelog Composer that synthesizes concise release notes by scoring profile deltas and surface-sampling representative examples.

What this delivers: - Affordance annotator: a modular analyzer that runs as part of the intake pipeline. It emits standardized tags such as "safety:high-sensitivity", "affordance:table-reading", "latency:snappy", "kvcache-friendly". Tags are stored on variant metadata and are consumed by routing heuristics and the release composer. - Delta-aware composer: a rule-and-score based engine that compares new vs baseline profiles (latency/throughput/semantic metrics) and outputs a compact changelog with: summary bullets, graded impact (minor/major/regressive), representative prompt-response pairs (sampled), and suggested mitigation notes (rollback candidate, alternative variant). - CLI preview: a new 'registry changelog preview' command that produces draft notes for reviewers; safe-by-default (no auto-merge) and opt-in automerge via policy.

Key implementation points: - Annotator uses an ensemble of heuristics: static signatures (model arch × tokenization), sampled perf probes, and lightweight semantic probes (subset of eval harness) to infer capability hints. - Affordance schema extends variant metadata with a small, namespaced map: "affordances.v1" (fields are additive and backward compatible). Migration: existing variants without affordances are treated as unknown and do not break routing. - Composer implements a delta scoring pipeline with tunable weights (latency, accuracy deltas, hallucination signal). It de-duplicates noise via clustering and ranks candidate bullets by estimated user impact. - Storage: changelog drafts are stored as compact artifacts with provenance links to commits, Linear IDs, and sampling seeds. - Security/compliance: no user prompt data is stored; representative examples are sanitized and sampled from synthetic probes or public datasets where applicable.

Commits (high level): - 1a2b3c: initial annotator scaffold, schema and unit tests - 2d3e4f: perf probe harness integration and sampling strategy - 3f4g5h: delta scoring engine and composer templates - 4h5i6j: CLI preview and documentation - 5i6j7k: migration script and end-to-end tests - 6j7k8l: polish, CI flakiness fixes, and sample sanitization

Files/areas changed (high level): - src/annotator/affordance/ : new annotator library, heuristics and unit tests - schemas/variant_affordances_v1.yaml : new compact schema extension - tools/cli/changelog_preview.py : CLI to preview composer output - composer/delta_engine.py : delta scoring and summarizer - ingest/pipeline_hooks.py : hook to run annotator on intake - migration/migrate_affordances.py : one-time additive migration - docs/onboarding/affordances.md : onboarding guide and examples - tests/e2e/changelog_flow.test : end-to-end flow using mock probes

Illustrative snippet (composer template):
{
  "summary": "Minor regression in table-answering latency (p95 +120ms), accuracy stable",
  "impact": "minor",
  "examples": ["(sanitized example prompt 1)", "(sanitized example prompt 2)"],
  "suggested_action": "Consider routing 'table-heavy' traffic to variant v2.1 until kvcache backfills match baseline"
}

Review conversation (condensed): - Maya Chen (reviewer): "Nice separation between annotator and composer. Two asks: 1) tighten sampling seed determinism for reproducibility, 2) add a short-circuit to skip semantic probes when running in air-gapped installs." - Author reply (Priya): "Addressed both. Sampling seed is now deterministic via registry seed service; semantic probe is gated behind a 'probe-mode' flag and disabled by default in private installs." - Diego Alvarez: "Please add a note about cost/latency trade-offs of running annotator in the hot path." - Author: "Moved heavy perf probes to async background workers; hot path keeps cheap static signatures only. Documented tradeoffs in affordances.md." - Samir Patel: "Approve after tests flake is resolved." - CI runs fixed and tests green. Final approval given.

CI summary: - unit: pass - e2e (mock probes): pass - lint: pass - integration (registry staging): pass after a rerun due to transient infra failure - total: 6/6 checks green

Backward compatibility and migration: - Schema extension is additive (affordances.v1). Existing consumers will ignore the new field if unrecognized. - Migration script annotates historical variants in batches; it's rate-limited and idempotent. - No public API signature changes.

Rollout plan: 1) Deploy annotator to staging with background-only mode (no auto-stamp). 2) Enable annotator for new onboardings and run migration on low-priority buckets. 3) Turn on composer preview for reviewers. 4) After one-week vetting, enable composer to emit changelog drafts automatically. 5) Monitor quality metrics (changelog acceptance rate, manual edits, false-positive regression tags) and rollback policy for 24/7 on-call.

Risk and mitigation: - Over-tagging: mitigated by conservative defaults and human-in-the-loop preview. - Probe cost: heavy probes run async and rate-limited; CLI preview uses cached artifacts. - Privacy: sanitized examples only; customers can opt-out of sampling.

Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main on 2024-11-12 after approvals and green CI. Linear references: ENG-2147, PM-511.

Notes for reviewers/maintainers: - If you need to tune composer weightings, see composer/config/weights.yaml. - For private installs, set PROBE_MODE=disabled to avoid network-based semantic probes. - The migration script can be run with --dry-run to inspect candidate variants before applying.

title: Add cohort-aware changelog enricher and perf baseline seeder
preview: Introduce a cohort-aware changelog enrichment pipeline and a perf baseline seeder to improve release notes quality and initial performance expectations for newly onboarded model variants. The change adds an enrichment worker that consumes sampled telemetry and onboarding probe results, computes sample-weighted deltas per cohort, and emits human-readable summary assertions and suggested baseline numbers for the release composer. A lightweight SQLite indexer records cohort sample weights and fast lookup keys to avoid recomputing on every run. The PR also adds a small CLI entrypoint (rmr changelog enrich) and a seed script that writes initial baseline entries into the model registry so downstream automation and dashboards have an initial reference. Motivation: reduce manual work for release authors, provide reproducible baseline numbers for canary rollouts, and improve changelog context for customers. No breaking API changes to existing onboard manifests; the enrichment is opt-in via registry config and feature flag.
text: Introduce a cohort-aware changelog enrichment pipeline and a perf baseline seeder to improve release notes quality and initial performance expectations for newly onboarded model variants. The change adds an enrichment worker that consumes sampled telemetry and onboarding probe results, computes sample-weighted deltas per cohort, and emits human-readable summary assertions and suggested baseline numbers for the release composer. A lightweight SQLite indexer records cohort sample weights and fast lookup keys to avoid recomputing on every run. The PR also adds a small CLI entrypoint (rmr changelog enrich) and a seed script that writes initial baseline entries into the model registry so downstream automation and dashboards have an initial reference. Motivation: reduce manual work for release authors, provide reproducible baseline numbers for canary rollouts, and improve changelog context for customers. No breaking API changes to existing onboard manifests; the enrichment is opt-in via registry config and feature flag.

title: Compact probe serializer and autotag rationalizer for churn-resistant changelogs
preview:
Context and motivation: The model onboarding pipeline currently emits verbose probe payloads and naive changelog diffs which cause noisy release notes and expensive downstream storage/transfer. This PR introduces a compact probe serializer (binary + content-addressed prefixes) and an autotag rationalizer that deterministically picks minimal, stable tags for model profiles. The goal is to reduce changelog noise, make release diffs smaller, and improve the stability of model identifiers across quick re-uploads.

What this changes (high level): 1) CompactProbeSerializer: a new module that implements a size-aware serializer for probe samples. It uses a small binary encoding (protobuf-lite) combined with reservoir sampling for long prompts and optional bloom-filtered feature sketches for heavy fields so repeated telemetry is deduped early. 2) Fingerprint suffixes: content-addressed 8-byte suffixes are appended to the onboard manifest name to avoid collisions while remaining human-friendly. 3) AutotagRationalizer: a deterministic tagging engine that reduces tag churn by preferring semantic tags (capabilities, size, quantization) and only adds variant tags when the delta crosses significance thresholds. 4) Changelog Compactor: a pipeline step that collapses repetitive probe-level diffs into summarized impact briefs and exposes a compact delta used by the release composer.

Implementation notes: - Serializer is opt-in via ONBOARD_COMPACT_SERIALIZER=true. Backwards-compatible: fallback to legacy JSON payloads if disabled. - Reservoir sampling window defaults to 128 tokens per sample and is configurable per-model. - Bloom sketch uses 4 hashes / 1024 bits by default, configurable. - Tag rationalizer rules are expressed as prioritized predicates; new predicates can be added via config without data migration. - Changelog compaction runs as a streaming reducer that emits a concise summary blob (gzip + CBOR) and small provenance ledger (signed SHA256 deltas).

Checklist: - [x] Compact serializer implementation + tests - [x] Autotag rationalizer + unit rules tests - [x] Changelog compactor + integration harness - [x] Migration safety (feature flag + backward compatibility) - [x] Perf benchmark added to perf-lab (summary included below)

Benchmarks summary (perf-lab): average payload size reduced ~78% for representative onboarding samples, network transfer time reduced ~60% in our staging region, and release-note digest sizes reduced by ~5x on median workloads. CPU overhead for the serializer was ~3% on the worker process; acceptable under dedicated capacity.

Compatibility and rollout: This change is gated behind a feature flag and rollout is staged per-environment: dev -> canary -> staging -> prod. No immediate migration required for existing model profiles. Once enabled, changelog previews in the console will show the compact summary and an opt-in "expanded diff" for auditors.

Related work: ENG-4981 (changelog compaction), ENG-5023 (probe sampling), see also docs/arch/onboard-compact.md (new doc added in PR).

title: Consolidate onboard commit-capsule and auto-synthesize release briefs
preview:
Motivation: onboarding currently scatters commit-level metadata, perf deltas, and release note fragments across artifacts and the model profile DB. That makes lightweight release summaries hard to generate and forces reviewers to cross-reference multiple sources. This change introduces a compact 'commit-capsule' blob that travels with a model profile (stored as signed metadata alongside the profile artifact) and a small pipeline to auto-synthesize concise release briefs from the capsule + perf deltas.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a commit-capsule format (minimal JSON) that contains: commit SHA, onboarding CLI user, dataset snapshot tag, top-level performance tokens (latency/throughput/embedding-l2), a short human author blurb, and a list of significant calibration flags (quantization/seq-len/kv-cache-behavior). 2) Extends the onboard CLI to emit the capsule and attach it to the profile upload payload. 3) Introduces a release-brief synthesizer service (local transformer-free template engine) that aggregates capsule metadata and latest profile diffs to produce a one-paragraph release brief plus a 3-line perf summary. 4) Migration: a migration job scans recent profiles (90 days) and attempts best-effort capsule backfill from commit messages, upload metadata, and CI artifacts. 5) Console: adds a compact 'release brief' field in the profile summary view and a download link for the capsule.

Checklist: - [x] Define capsule schema and stability guarantee. - [x] Implement CLI emit and upload hooks. - [x] Implement synthesizer service and unit tests. - [x] Add console rendering and download endpoint. - [x] Add migration job and monitoring. - [x] Add Linear tickets and docs.

Backward compatibility: capsule is additive; profiles without capsules remain readable. Synthesizer falls back to a heuristic brief if capsule missing. Signed capsules use existing model-registry signing keys; no KMS changes required.

Rollout plan: merge to main; enable synthesizer feature-flag by default in staging for 48 hours, monitor release_brief_missing_ratio and brief_length regressions. Run migration job incrementally (10% windows) to avoid API spikes.

Related: ENG-1423 (onboard metadata reliability), PM-89 (concise release notes for partner-facing changelogs).

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the synthesizer lightweight (no heavy NLP models) to ensure predictable output and cost. Future follow-ups can add an optional LLM-based richer summarizer behind a feature flag.

title: Orchestrate onboarding DAG and release assembler for synthesized perf profiles
preview: Motivation: onboarding several new open models exposed a gap in how we compose metadata transforms, perf fragments, and release notes into a single deterministic model version artifact. This PR introduces an onboarding DAG (graph runner) that composes validators, normalizers, profile synthesizers, and a release-assembler that produces canonical changelog/release-note entries for each model version. What changed: - new onboarding/graph package (DAG runner, node interfaces, light scheduler) - deterministic version hash: canonical serializer + version salt derived from provenance - perf profile synthesizer: merges infra benchmark fragments, manual annotations, and quantization notes into a single profile document with provenance traces - release-assembler: template-driven changelog entries + optional GitHub release draft action - CLI hooks and GitHub Action integration (onboard:run, onboard:migrate) - migration shim to translate existing model profiles to the new canonical format - docs: onboarding guide, schema reference, and migration notes Checklist: - [x] Add DAG runner and node interfaces - [x] Implement deterministic hashing and serializer - [x] Add perf profile synthesizer and provenance fields - [x] Implement release-assembler and GitHub Action - [x] Add unit+integration tests and CI jobs - [x] Add docs and migration guide - [x] Update SDK examples to consume synthesized profiles Backwards compatibility and migration: existing profile documents are converted via a one-time migration CLI (onboard:migrate) that emits a preview bundle and a dry-run mode. We keep compatibility by preserving legacy fields under a legacy_metadata namespace and by allowing consumers to opt into the new canonical version hash. Performance expectations: profile synthesis is a CPU-bound transformation that adds ~15–40ms per onboarding job in our benchmark harness (depends on profile size). Related work: ENG-4122, ENG-4198, PM-703. See inline commits and tests for microbenchmarks and graphs.

title: chronicle-writer: compact onboarding abstracts and deterministic release zipper
preview: Context: onboarding churn and release-note noise slow cross-team reviews. Large profile uploads include verbose provenance and full sample dumps; downstream consumers only need a small, deterministic artifact to verify compatibility and pull release summaries. This PR introduces the 'chronicle-writer' pipeline which emits a compact, deterministic zip (the 'chronicle') containing: a lightweight onboarding abstract (machine + human friendly), a sampled provenance manifest (configurable seed & reservoir size), delta fingerprints for fast diffs, and a concise release index used by downstream syncers. Motivation: speed up reviews, reduce artifact size for audits, and enable deterministic release replays for regressions. What this PR changes: 1) chronicle service: new CLI and server handler to build deterministic zips from profile uploads; 2) compact-onboard-abstract: a templated, schema-backed one-page summary derived from model metadata + top-k perf tokens; 3) sampled-provenance: reservoir sampler integrated into the onboard pipeline with seed control to ensure reproducible samples; 4) zipper: deterministic packaging/ordering + SHA256 manifest and timestamping; 5) release-sync helpers: small client library to fetch and validate chronicles and apply delta patches. Compatibility notes: chronicles are opt-in at onboarding and enabled by default for curated workflows; they are additive and do not replace existing full-profile bundles. Checklist: - [x] CLI + server handlers implemented - [x] deterministic ordering + manifest hashing - [x] reservoir sampling with seed control - [x] tests for determinism under varying node counts - [x] basic docs and QuickStart snippet - [x] migration script to backfill chronicles for recent 30 days Rollout plan: merge behind feature flag chronicle.enabled=false, enable for curated bucket the next day, observe storage and review latency for 72h, then flip default for new uploads. Related work: ties to ENG-8842 (release UX) and the release zipper RFC (internal link).

title: Affinity-guided changelog composer and manifest affinity stamps
preview: Motivation: make variant rollouts and capacity planning faster by surfacing runtime affinity signals on model manifests and by auto-drafting concise, priority-sorted changelog blurbs. This PR adds an opt-in 'affinity_stamp' metadata to manifests (hardware_family, os_kernel_tag, expected_latency_bucket), a small composer service that merges onboarding diffs with occupancy and sample-sufficiency heuristics to produce release snippets, and a migration/backfill tool that infers affinity from recent perf snapshots. The composer emits short human-readable blurbs and structured 'rollout_advisory' labels that downstream SRE and release automation can consume. Backwards compatibility: manifests without affinity_stamp are accepted and treated as 'affinity: unknown'. Feature-flagged rollout with dry-run and canary namespace. Linear links: ENG-10111 and ENG-9142. Related to automated release notes initiative ENG-8723 and SRE request SUP-321.
